Issue an alarm or alert in response to
detected unauthorized access through a
physical access point into a Physical
Security Perimeter to the personnel
identified in the BES Cyber Security
Incident response plan within 15 minutes
of detection.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Monitor each Physical Access Control
System for unauthorized physical access
to a Physical Access Control System.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Issue an alarm or alert in response to
detected unauthorized physical access to
a Physical Access Control System to the
personnel identified in the BES Cyber
Security Incident response plan within 15
minutes of the detection.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Log (through automated means or by
personnel who control entry) entry of
each individual with authorized
unescorted physical access into each
Physical Security Perimeter, with
information to identify the individual and
date and time of entry.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Retain physical access logs of entry of
individuals with authorized unescorted
physical access into each Physical Security
Perimeter for at least ninety calendar
days.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Restrict physical access to cabling and other
nonprogrammable communication
components used for connection between
applicable Cyber Assets within the same
Electronic Security Perimeter in those
instances when such cabling and components
are located outside of a Physical Security
Perimeter.
Where physical access restrictions to such
cabling and components are not
implemented, the Responsible Entity shall
document and implement one or more of the
following:
 encryption of data that transits such
cabling and components; or
 monitoring the status of the
communication link composed of
such cabling and components and
issuing an alarm or alert in response
to detected communication failures
to the personnel identified in the BES
Cyber Security Incident response plan
within 15 minutes of detection; or
 an equally effective logical
protection.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust cryptographic protections and network segmentation capabilities to secure data in transit across its internal networks. All VCF components utilize FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules, with NSX including multiple validated modules through the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program. TLS 1.3 is the default for internal VCF communications, with TLS 1.2 as a compatibility fallback, and all system management network communications are encrypted by default using standardized cipher suites. VCF Operations provides centralized certificate lifecycle management for all VCF components, enabling automated certificate renewal, monitoring, and replacement.

Data-in-transit encryption is extensively supported, including vSAN data-in-transit encryption using AES-256 for all data and metadata traffic between hosts, enforcing forward secrecy. Encrypted vSphere vMotion protects virtual machine data during live migration, with configurable modes to enforce encryption policy. Network traffic separation is supported through distributed switch models, such as Storage Separation and Workload Separation, which can provide physical air-gapped isolation of network traffic onto separate physical paths.

VMware Live Recovery mandates TLS 1.2 for all replication traffic between protected and recovery sites, supports the replication of encrypted virtual machines, and uses certificate-based trust for site pairing. VMware vDefend secures communication between its product components using certificates and secure TLS versions with approved cipher suites, encrypts stored data using AES 256-bit, and provides TLS Inspection capabilities for network traffic. These capabilities ensure that even if physical cabling is compromised, the encrypted and segmented traffic remains protected from unauthorized access.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive technical controls for data encryption and logical protection, the initial requirement to restrict physical access to cabling and nonprogrammable communication components remains an organizational and facility responsibility. This includes implementing physical security measures such as conduits, locked pathways, and controlled access to wiring closets. The organization is responsible for establishing and enforcing policies and procedures for physical access controls. If physical access restrictions are not implemented, the organization must document and implement one or more of the specified alternatives, including the configuration and ongoing management of encryption, or the implementation of communication link status monitoring and alarming within 15 minutes of detection, along with the associated incident response plan.

Description:
Require continuous escorted access of
visitors (individuals who are provided
access but are not authorized for
unescorted physical access) within each
Physical Security Perimeter, except during
CIP Exceptional Circumstances.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Require manual or automated logging of
visitor entry into and exit from the Physical
Security Perimeter that includes date and
time of the initial entry and last exit, the
visitor’s name, and the name of an
individual point of contact responsible for
the visitor, except during CIP Exceptional
Circumstances.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Retain visitor logs for at least ninety
calendar days.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive, configurable log retention capabilities across its integrated components, enabling organizations to meet requirements for retaining visitor logs for at least ninety calendar days. VCF Operations for Logs serves as the centralized log management platform, allowing administrators to define retention periods for operational and audit log data, including critical audit events such as user logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications. VCF Automation also offers specific settings for retaining activity log history, and ESX hosts support configurable log file rotation to maintain a specified number of log files. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further contributes by providing configurable log retention mechanisms for disaster recovery operations and recovery plan history. VMware vDefend retains security event data generated by its features for defined periods, adding to the overall logging capabilities. All VCF components, including VLR, support syslog forwarding to external log management systems and SIEM platforms, which is a standard approach for centralized retention policy enforcement and meeting long-term or specific statutory, regulatory, and contractual retention requirements.

The customer is responsible for defining what constitutes "visitor logs" within their specific operational context and for configuring the retention settings across VCF components and any integrated external log management solutions to ensure these identified logs are retained for the required ninety-day period.

Description:
Maintenance and testing of each Physical
Access Control System and locally mounted
hardware or devices at the Physical
Security Perimeter at least once every 24
calendar months to ensure they function
properly.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall implement one or more documented physical security plan(s) that collectively include all of
the applicable requirement parts in CIP‐006‐6 Table R1 – Physical Security Plan. [Violation Risk Factor: Medium] [Time
Horizon: Long Term Planning and Same Day Operations]
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall implement one or more documented visitor control program(s) that include each of the
applicable requirement parts in CIP‐006‐6 Table R2 – Visitor Control Program. [Violation Risk Factor: Medium] [Time Horizon:
Same Day Operations.]
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall implement one or more documented Physical Access Control System maintenance and testing
program(s) that collectively include each of the applicable requirement parts in CIP‐006‐6 Table R3 – Maintenance and Testing
Program. [Violation Risk Factor: Medium] [Time Horizon: Long Term Planning].
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust configuration-state review mechanisms that contribute to the maintenance of technology assets, including the underlying IT infrastructure supporting Physical Access Control Systems. VCF Operations continuously monitors the infrastructure against industry-standard security benchmarks and custom policies, calculating compliance scores for managed objects. This ensures that the virtual machines, hosts, and network components comprising or interacting with a PACS adhere to defined cybersecurity and data protection policies. vSphere Configuration Profiles and vSphere Lifecycle Manager assess ESX host configurations against approved baselines, detecting and alerting on drift from desired states, with options for automatic remediation. NSX provides compliance status reporting for network configurations, ensuring the network segments used by PACS components remain compliant.

VMware vDefend further contributes by providing network security-specific compliance assessment, policy analysis, and segmentation scoring capabilities for the network components of a PACS. The Security Services Platform includes a Security Segmentation score and report generation, evaluating microsegmentation policy enforcement. Its metrics service collects operational data across vDefend components like Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and IDS/IPS, which are critical for securing PACS network communications. Security Intelligence offers policy recommendation analysis, identifying gaps between desired and actual network security policies. The Threat Event Monitoring dashboard provides visibility into detected threats and policy violations across the data center, creating an evidence trail for compliance reviews.

While these platforms provide comprehensive tools for maintaining the configuration compliance and network security of the IT infrastructure that supports a Physical Access Control System, they do not directly manage or functionally test the physical access control mechanisms themselves, such as door locks, card readers, or physical intrusion detection systems. Organizations must define and implement a documented maintenance and testing program that specifically addresses the physical components of the PACS, including periodic functional validation and controlled test scenarios to ensure these physical controls operate as intended. The organization is responsible for establishing the policies, procedures, and human accountability required for this comprehensive program.

Description:
Where technically feasible, enable only
logical network accessible ports that
have been determined to be needed by
the Responsible Entity, including port
ranges or services where needed to
handle dynamic ports. If a device has
no provision for disabling or restricting
logical ports on the device then those
ports that are open are deemed
needed.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide comprehensive, layered mechanisms to enforce least functionality by restricting logical network accessible ports, protocols, and services.

ESX hosts incorporate a built-in firewall that controls service access, supporting specification of allowed hosts and individual service management, including lockdown mode for centralized vCenter management. VCF further enables disabling unexposed features through isolation configuration parameters and isolates management traffic on dedicated, VLAN-backed distributed port groups.

At the network layer, VCF utilizes distributed switches with port group security policies and VLAN IDs to segment traffic, while VCF Operations collects ESX security properties and automatically configures required ports for services like vSAN, ensuring firewall rules align with actual service needs.

Firewall hardening through VCF Operations restricts internal service access, and NSX allows for precise service definition with port and protocol pairings for use in firewall rules. Distributed port groups also support SpoofGuard and switch security profiles to prevent traffic spoofing.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with its Distributed Firewall, operating at the vNIC level to enforce granular allow, drop, or reject rules based on specific ports, protocols, and even application identity (Layer 2 through Layer 7 filtering). These rules are processed across multiple policy sections, providing fine-grained control over traffic categories.

The Security Services Platform enhances this by offering rule optimization analysis to identify and flag overly permissive, redundant, or ineffective rules, directly supporting a least-privilege security model. Traffic flow analysis with protocol and port filtering provides visibility to verify essential communication and detect unexpected patterns.

Furthermore, segmentation planning capabilities allow for defining broad block policies between environment pairs, and the Gateway Firewall applies similar allow, drop, and reject actions at the perimeter of logical network segments, creating a robust, layered enforcement of essential network access.

While the platform provides the robust technical controls and visibility to restrict network access to only essential ports and services, the Responsible Entity must define and determine which specific logical network accessible ports, protocols, and services are genuinely needed for their applications and operational environments. This includes establishing the organizational policies, processes, and accountability for identifying, documenting, configuring, and continuously monitoring these requirements to ensure that only authorized and necessary network functionality is enabled.

Description:
Protect against the use of unnecessary
physical input/output ports used for
network connectivity, console
commands, or Removable Media.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms to protect against the use of unnecessary input/output ports within the virtualized environment. Administrators possess granular control over the virtual I/O devices presented to virtual machines, enabling the removal of unnecessary components such as virtual USB controllers, CD/DVD drives, serial and parallel ports, and floppy drives. This capability serves as the direct virtualized equivalent of physically removing unnecessary ports from a bare-metal system.

To further enhance security, advanced settings like `isolation.device.connectable.disable` prevent unauthorized connection or removal of devices at runtime, while `isolation.device.edit.disable` prohibits unauthorized modification of device configurations. The `VirtualDeviceConnectInfo.allowGuestControl` property ensures that the guest operating system cannot alter a device's connection state, thereby preventing users within a VM from re-enabling devices that have been intentionally disconnected by administrators.

For workloads requiring direct access to physical hardware, DirectPath I/O passthrough is explicitly configured on a per-VM and per-device basis, ensuring that physical PCI/PCIe devices are exposed only to virtual machines with a documented and approved requirement. Access is subsequently revoked by removing the virtual device backing from the VM configuration.

VCF Operations continuously monitors these device isolation settings as part of its compliance benchmarks, flagging virtual machines where device controls are not configured according to established policy, providing ongoing visibility into potential vulnerabilities from unnecessary I/O paths.

While VMware Cloud Foundation delivers the technical controls and monitoring capabilities to enforce I/O device isolation, the customer is responsible for establishing and implementing organizational policies that define what constitutes an "unnecessary" I/O port for specific workloads and security profiles. This includes the administrative action of configuring virtual machines to remove unneeded virtual devices and applying the necessary advanced settings.

Customers must also maintain documentation for any workloads requiring DirectPath I/O passthrough and ensure that VCF Operations monitoring alerts regarding non-compliant device configurations are addressed promptly according to established security policies.

Description:
A patch management process for
tracking, evaluating, and installing
cyber security patches for applicable
Cyber Assets. The tracking portion
shall include the identification of a
source or sources that the
Responsible Entity tracks for the
release of cyber security patches for
applicable Cyber Assets that are
updateable and for which a patching
source exists.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides integrated capabilities for tracking, evaluating, and installing cybersecurity patches across its entire infrastructure stack. VCF Operations includes lifecycle management tools and a patch planner that identifies available updates for VCF core components, downloads binaries from online depots, and orchestrates their installation.

Pre-update validation performs health checks and compatibility verification before applying changes, ensuring system stability.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages the desired-state configuration for ESX hosts, including firmware and drivers through Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins, scanning hosts for deviations and remediating non-compliant systems.

Broadcom publishes security advisories and releases patches for VCF vulnerabilities, which are then managed and deployed through these integrated tools.

The vCenter patching API supports discovery of all available and applicable updates, and PowerCLI provides cmdlets for downloading patches, creating baselines, and scanning or remediating hosts and virtual machines. VCF supports staged rollouts, rollback capabilities, and Live Patching for discrete components, ensuring operational continuity.

The VCF Download Tool facilitates patch management in offline environments.

VMware vDefend complements these patching capabilities by providing network-level compensating controls and enhanced visibility into potential exploitation attempts.

Its Distributed IDS/IPS detects exploitation attempts against known vulnerabilities in unpatched systems, allowing for virtual patching through custom signatures to block exploit traffic before official patches are deployed.

vDefend's Security Intelligence and posture dashboards offer insights into network security and threat events, helping security teams prioritize patching efforts based on observed activity. Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) extends detection to include behavioral analysis and sandboxing, providing an additional layer of protection against zero-day exploits for systems awaiting patches.

While VCF provides the technical mechanisms for patch deployment and lifecycle management, the customer is responsible for formally defining and documenting their comprehensive patch management process, including the specific sources they track for patch releases, their evaluation criteria, and the assignment of human accountability for these activities.

Description:
A patch management process for
tracking, evaluating, and installing
cyber security patches for applicable
Cyber Assets. The tracking portion
shall include the identification of a
source or sources that the
Responsible Entity tracks for the
release of cyber security patches for
applicable Cyber Assets that are
updateable and for which a patching
source exists.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides integrated vulnerability and patch management capabilities across its entire infrastructure stack, directly addressing the requirements for tracking, evaluating, and installing cyber security patches. VCF Operations includes lifecycle management tools that identify available updates for core components, download binaries from designated depots, and orchestrate their installation across the stack. Pre-update validation ensures system health and compatibility before changes are applied.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages the desired-state configuration for ESX hosts, including firmware and drivers via Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins, scanning for deviations and remediating non-compliant hosts. The Update Manager module in PowerCLI further enables programmatic patch downloading, baseline creation, and host/virtual machine remediation.

VCF Operations compliance management continuously monitors for policy violations, highlights risks, and offers actionable remediation, with options for automated remediation. Broadcom publishes security advisories for VCF vulnerabilities and releases patches, serving as the identified source for security updates.

VMware vDefend significantly contributes to the overall vulnerability management posture by providing network-layer detection, virtual patching, and security posture monitoring capabilities that complement traditional patch management workflows.

The Distributed IDS/IPS offers signature-based detection of vulnerability exploitation attempts across east-west traffic, enabling the deployment of custom signatures for virtual patching to block exploits before official patches are available. The IDS/IPS dashboard provides critical visibility into active threats.

Security Intelligence and the Security Services Platform offer comprehensive security posture dashboards, threat event monitoring, and the Security Explorer for visualizing workload exposure, aiding in the prioritization of remediation efforts. Advanced Threat Prevention extends these capabilities with behavioral analysis and sandboxing, providing an additional layer of protection against malware and zero-day exploits for systems awaiting patches.

While VCF and vDefend provide the comprehensive technical framework and automation for patch management, the customer is responsible for defining the overarching patch management process.

This includes establishing specific organizational policies, approval workflows, maintenance windows, and assigning human accountability for overseeing the process, evaluating patches, and responding to alerts generated by the platform.

Description:
At least once every 35 calendar days,
evaluate security patches for
applicability that have been released
since the last evaluation from the
source or sources identified in Part
2.1.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive, integrated lifecycle management tools that continuously evaluate security patches for applicability across the infrastructure stack. VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically detects known issues and vulnerabilities within the VCF software stack, providing root cause insights and actionable remediation steps. The compliance management capability continuously monitors infrastructure, identifies non-compliant objects, and provides remediation recommendations, effectively assessing the applicability of necessary security updates. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages desired-state configurations for ESX hosts, applying remediation when hosts deviate from the target specification, which includes patch levels. Configuration Profiles and Image-Based Lifecycle Management periodically assess host configurations, alert on drift, and offer automated remediation, ensuring that the environment is continuously evaluated against desired security baselines. Pre-update validation performs health checks and compatibility verification before updates are applied, directly contributing to the evaluation of patch applicability. Broadcom publishes security advisories for known vulnerabilities, which serve as the authoritative source for VCF's integrated vulnerability intelligence.

VMware vDefend contributes to overall vulnerability management by providing network-level threat detection and automated response mechanisms that identify and contain active threats. Its Distributed IDS/IPS continuously monitors east-west traffic for known vulnerability exploits, and the Security Services Platform enables administrators to apply remediation actions to block attack vectors. This capability acts as a critical compensating control, mitigating the exploitation of vulnerabilities in real-time while patches are being evaluated and deployed.

While VCF automates the technical evaluation of patch applicability, customers are responsible for defining their specific patch sources as identified in Part 2.1, configuring desired state baselines, and establishing the organizational processes for reviewing and approving the automated remediation recommendations.

Description:
For applicable patches identified in
Part 2.2, within 35 calendar days of
the evaluation completion, take one
of the following actions:
 Apply the applicable patches; or
 Create a dated mitigation plan;
or
 Revise an existing mitigation
plan.
Mitigation plans shall include the
Responsible Entity’s planned actions
to mitigate the vulnerabilities
addressed by each security patch and
a timeframe to complete these
mitigations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive, integrated lifecycle management for identifying, tracking, and remediating vulnerabilities across the entire infrastructure stack. VCF Operations orchestrates centralized lifecycle management, including pre-update validation, update bundle management, and component interdependency management to ensure successful patch application.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages desired-state configurations for ESX hosts, applying remediation when hosts deviate from the target specification, and extends to firmware and driver management via Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins.

VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically detects known issues and vulnerabilities, providing root cause insights and actionable remediation steps.

The compliance management capability continuously monitors infrastructure, identifies non-compliant objects, and offers automated remediation actions configurable based on severity, bringing objects back into compliance without manual intervention.

VCF supports staged rollouts, rollback capabilities, and Live Patching for discrete components, ensuring operational continuity during updates.

VMware vDefend further contributes to vulnerability remediation by providing network-level threat detection and automated response mechanisms that identify, track, and mitigate security events across protected workloads. The Distributed IDS/IPS continuously monitors east-west traffic for known vulnerability exploits, logging events and allowing for custom signature deployment to address zero-day vulnerabilities or custom applications, effectively providing virtual patching. The Security Services Platform offers a structured remediation workflow, enabling administrators to apply actions that automatically create Distributed Firewall rules or IDS/IPS policies to block attack vectors. These capabilities serve as critical compensating controls, reducing exploitation risk during the window between vulnerability disclosure and patch deployment by detecting and containing threats in real-time.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide the technical capabilities for applying patches and implementing robust technical mitigations, the formal process of creating or revising a dated mitigation plan, including the definition of the Responsible Entity’s planned actions and a specific timeframe for completion, remains a customer responsibility. The platform provides the tools to execute the technical actions that would be documented within such a plan, but it does not automate the generation, dating, or formal management of these administrative planning documents.

Description:
For each mitigation plan created or
revised in Part 2.3, implement the
plan within the timeframe specified in
the plan, unless a revision to the plan
or an extension to the timeframe
specified in Part 2.3 is approved by
the CIP Senior Manager or delegate.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for identifying, tracking, and remediating vulnerabilities across the infrastructure stack. VCF Operations orchestrates centralized lifecycle management, including pre-update validation, update bundle management, and component interdependency management, supporting staged rollouts and rollback. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages desired-state configurations for ESX hosts, applying remediation when deviations occur, extended by Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins for firmware and drivers. VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically detects known issues and vulnerabilities, providing root cause insights and actionable remediation steps. The compliance management capability continuously monitors infrastructure, identifies non-compliant objects, and can be configured for automated remediation actions based on severity, bringing objects back into compliance without manual intervention. Configuration Profiles and Image-Based Lifecycle Management periodically assess host configurations, alert on drift, and offer automated remediation. VCF tracks configuration changes to support compliance reporting. Live Patching and Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) support zero-downtime maintenance during update operations, facilitating timely remediation.

VMware vDefend contributes to vulnerability remediation by providing network-level threat detection and automated response mechanisms. Its Distributed IDS/IPS continuously monitors east-west traffic for known vulnerability exploits, logging events and allowing for custom signature deployment to address new vulnerabilities. The Security Services Platform offers a structured remediation workflow, enabling administrators to apply actions that automatically create Distributed Firewall rules or IDS/IPS policies to block attack vectors. Intelligent Assist automates the selection between targeted and comprehensive remediation strategies and handles the creation of necessary security objects.

While VCF and vDefend provide robust technical tools for identifying vulnerabilities and executing remediation actions, the customer retains responsibility for defining the specific mitigation plans, establishing the timeframes for their implementation, and managing the formal approval process for any plan revisions or extensions to those timeframes by a CIP Senior Manager or delegate. Specifically, vDefend remediations require administrators to review and manually apply suggested actions through the NSX Manager UI, necessitating customer operational procedures and human accountability to ensure timely implementation. The overall adherence to specified timeframes and the management of approval workflows for deviations are policy and process-driven requirements that reside with the customer.

Description:
Deploy method(s) to deter, detect, or
prevent malicious code.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust foundation for deterring and preventing malicious code through platform integrity controls. ESX hosts leverage Secure Boot and code signing to prevent unauthorized code execution, complemented by Trusted Platform Module (TPM) support and host attestation via vCenter to ensure a known good configuration. Virtual machine Secure Boot through UEFI provides guest operating system integrity verification, and VMware Tools packages are cryptographically signed and verified. vSphere Lifecycle Manager and Configuration Profiles maintain desired-state configuration for ESX hosts, detecting and remediating drift to identify unauthorized software modifications. VCF Operations actively monitors the security state, alerting on anomalies. For containerized workloads, Harbor registry integration provides image signing, vulnerability scanning, and content trust, preventing the deployment of compromised images.

VMware vDefend significantly enhances detection and prevention capabilities with its Malware Prevention Service, IDS/IPS, and network detection and response. The Malware Prevention Service, deployed as a service virtual machine, inspects files and data streams in real-time, combining signature-based detection with behavioral analysis to identify known and zero-day threats, including fileless malware operating in memory. It classifies threats, assigns verdicts, and provides dynamic analysis for forensic insight. vDefend's distributed IDS/IPS provides signature-based and behavioral intrusion detection and prevention at the virtual network interface level, detecting network-borne malware delivery, command-and-control communications, and lateral movement across east-west traffic. URL filtering and the Gateway Firewall block access to known malicious sites, while the Malware Prevention service inspects files transferred over various protocols, assigning verdicts and optionally submitting files to a cloud sandbox for deeper analysis. The Malware Prevention Dashboard centralizes detection events, enabling security teams to track outbreaks and coordinate efforts.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide extensive technical mechanisms for deterring, detecting, and preventing malicious code at the infrastructure and network layers, full eradication of malicious code from within an already-infected guest operating system typically requires dedicated endpoint security tools operating directly within the guest. Furthermore, the platform's network-level capabilities for malicious link and file download vectors do not directly extend to email clients or collaboration platform endpoints. Customers are responsible for implementing and managing these additional endpoint security solutions and for establishing comprehensive organizational policies and processes to govern the use and response to these technical controls.

Description:
Mitigate the threat of detected
malicious code.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust foundation for mitigating malicious code threats through platform integrity controls.

ESX hosts enforce Secure Boot and code signing, leveraging Trusted Platform Module (TPM) support and host attestation to ensure a known good configuration. Virtual machine Secure Boot via UEFI verifies guest operating system integrity, and VMware Tools packages are cryptographically signed. vSphere Lifecycle Manager and Configuration Profiles maintain desired-state configuration for ESX hosts, detecting and remediating drift to prevent unauthorized modifications. For containerized workloads, Harbor registry integration provides image signing, vulnerability scanning, and content trust, preventing the deployment of compromised images.

VMware vDefend Firewall with Advanced Threat Prevention extends these capabilities by providing distributed malware detection and prevention, IDS/IPS, and network detection and response for virtualized workloads.

The vDefend Malware Prevention Service, deployed as a service virtual machine, inspects files and data streams in real-time, utilizing signature-based and behavioral analysis to detect both known and zero-day threats, including fileless malware. It collects forensic evidence and classifies threats, while vDefend IDS/IPS provides signature-based and behavioral intrusion detection at the distributed firewall level, identifying network-borne malware, command-and-control communications, and lateral movement. The Malware Prevention Dashboard offers centralized visibility into detection events, enabling security teams to track outbreaks and coordinate responses.

While vDefend provides strong capabilities to detect and prevent the spread of malicious code across the network and block known threats from reaching workloads, full eradication of malicious code from an already infected guest operating system typically requires dedicated endpoint security tools operating within the guest. Organizations must implement comprehensive security policies, incident response procedures, and integrate additional host-level remediation tooling to achieve complete eradication and fully mitigate the threat within guest operating systems.

Description:
For those methods identified in Part
3.1 that use signatures or patterns,
have a process for the update of the
signatures or patterns. The process
must address testing and installing the
signatures or patterns.
Response:
VMware vDefend, an add-on to VMware Cloud Foundation, provides robust signature-based detection capabilities through its Malware Prevention Service and Intrusion Detection/Prevention System (IDS/IPS). The Malware Prevention Service inspects files and data streams in real time, leveraging signature-based detection to identify known malicious content. The vDefend IDS/IPS applies signature-based rules at the distributed firewall level to detect network-borne malware delivery attempts, command-and-control communications, and lateral movement. Organizations can upload and manage custom signature bundles within vDefend IDS/IPS to extend detection coverage for specific threats. Furthermore, Harbor registry integration within VCF provides vulnerability scanning for container images, which relies on patterns and definitions to identify known vulnerabilities.

While VMware vDefend provides the technical mechanisms for signature-based detection and the management of custom signature bundles, the establishment of a formal process for the update, testing, and installation of all signatures and patterns, including those provided by the vendor and those developed internally, remains the responsibility of the customer. The platform facilitates the deployment and use of these signatures, but the overarching procedural controls for their lifecycle management, particularly the testing phase, must be defined and implemented by the organization.

VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive logging capabilities across its components, capturing the necessary event types for security incident identification and investigation. VCF Operations audit logs record successful and failed login attempts, as well as other access and account management events, with explicit success and failure states. These logs include critical information such as user identity, timestamps, event source, and outcome, enabling detailed analysis.

VMware vDefend further enhances this by logging detected malicious code through its Malware Prevention Service, which categorizes file analysis events by detection status and verdict. The Distributed IDS/IPS generates detection events based on signature matches, and Network Detection and Response (NDR) identifies network threats, all contributing to the detection and logging of malicious activity. Additionally, vDefend's Distributed Firewall logs record dropped or rejected traffic, directly addressing failed access attempts.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) also contributes by generating error-level events for failed data protection operations, providing insight into unsuccessful access attempts related to recovery processes. All these logs are structured with event types, timestamps, and source information, and are centrally accessible through VCF Operations for Logs, which offers advanced search and correlation features to support after-the-fact investigations.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms for logging these events and generating alerts, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing the specific policies, procedures, and human processes required for incident identification and after-the-fact investigations. This includes configuring alert thresholds, establishing incident response workflows, and integrating the platform's logging and alerting capabilities with their broader security operations center (SOC) and incident management systems.

Description:
Log events at the BES Cyber System level (per BES Cyber System capability) or at the Cyber Asset level (per Cyber Asset capability) for identification of, and after-the-fact investigations of, Cyber Security Incidents that includes, as a minimum, each of the following types of events:
4.1.1. Detected successful login attempts;
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components generate comprehensive event logs that explicitly capture successful login attempts. VCF Operations categorizes audit events, including those related to Access, Access_control, and Account_management, and records both success and failure states for operations across the platform. These logs include essential details such as user identity (User ID, User Name, Auth Source), timestamps, event source (e.g., ESX host, syslog forwarder), and outcome. The VCF Operations for Logs Analyze page provides a centralized interface for searching, filtering, and correlating these events, enabling effective identification and investigation of security incidents.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) and VMware vDefend further enrich the audit trail by logging user-attributed actions and configuration changes. VLR records permission audit events with user identity, role, and affected entities, as well as recovery plan execution events with status. vDefend captures user identity for administrative configuration changes in NSX Manager audit logs and associates network traffic with Active Directory users via Identity Firewall, providing context for actions performed post-login. These capabilities ensure that not only are successful logins recorded, but subsequent user activities and system changes are also logged with sufficient detail for comprehensive after-the-fact investigations.

Description:
Log events at the BES Cyber System level (per BES Cyber System capability) or at the Cyber Asset level (per Cyber Asset capability) for identification of, and after-the-fact investigations of, Cyber Security Incidents that includes, as a minimum, each of the following types of events:

4.1.2. Detected failed access attempts and failed login attempts;
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation produces comprehensive event logs across its components, capturing detected failed access attempts and failed login attempts. VCF Operations audit events are categorized, including Access, Access_control, and Account_management, and explicitly record success and failure states for operations across the platform. Each event includes a timestamp, event source, and user identity, allowing for precise identification of who attempted what, when, and where.

The Distributed Firewall within vDefend logs traffic that is dropped or rejected, directly indicating failed network access attempts. These logs are enriched with timestamps, source details, and the outcome of the rule evaluation. VMware Live Recovery also generates error-level events for failed operations and records permission changes, contributing to the overall audit trail of system interactions.

The VCF Operations for Logs Analyze page provides a centralized interface for searching and correlating these log events across all sources, with filtering by timestamp, text, source, and extracted fields to support investigation and analysis.

Description:
Log events at the BES Cyber System level (per BES Cyber System capability) or at the Cyber Asset level (per Cyber Asset capability) for identification of, and after-the-fact investigations of, Cyber Security Incidents that includes, as a minimum, each of the following types of events:

4.1.3. Detected malicious code.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for logging detected malicious code events across its integrated security services.

The platform's Malware Prevention Service logs file analysis events, categorizing them by detection status and verdict, explicitly identifying malicious code. The Intrusion Detection/Prevention System (IDS/IPS) generates detection events based on signature matches, categorized by severity and threat, indicating potential malicious activity. Network Detection and Response (NDR) further generates detection events with structured metadata identifying various network threats.

These logs consistently include essential information for incident identification and after-the-fact investigations, such as precise timestamps, the specific event source (e.g., client VM ID, vNIC, network segment, affected workload), the outcome of the security operation (e.g., malicious verdict, alert/block action, severity/confidence level), and user identity where applicable (e.g., through Identity Firewall or NSX Manager audit logs for configuration changes).

The VCF Operations for Logs Analyze page provides a centralized interface for searching, filtering, and correlating these diverse log events across the platform, supporting detailed analysis.

While the platform provides the robust technical mechanisms for logging detected malicious code, the customer is responsible for enabling and configuring the security services, defining specific security policies and custom IDS signatures, and integrating these logs with their Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems to centralize, analyze, and respond to these logged events as part of their overall Cyber Security Incident Response Plan.

Description:
Generate alerts for security events that the Responsible Entity determines necessitates an alert, that includes, as a minimum, each of the following types of events (per Cyber Asset or BES Cyber System capability):
4.2.1. Detected malicious code from Part 4.1; and
4.2.2. Detected failure of Part 4.1 event logging.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides an integrated framework for alert generation, correlation, and response across the infrastructure stack. VCF Operations generates alerts based on collected data, including performance metrics, log events, or custom conditions, allowing administrators to define specific symptoms and prioritize responses. Intelligent Alerts group related events, aiding in root cause analysis. VCF Operations for Logs collects comprehensive log data from all VCF components, enabling the creation of log-based alert definitions that trigger notifications when specific patterns, such as those indicating malicious code or logging failures, appear. Outbound plug-in instances support integration with external systems for alert distribution via email, SNMP traps, REST webhooks, or log files. VMware vDefend further contributes by providing a Distributed IDS/IPS that generates alerts for detected network threats, directly addressing malicious code detection within its security domain. VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities with DR-specific alarms and notifications, integrating into the broader vCenter alarm framework.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms for alert generation and notification, the Responsible Entity must define the specific security events that necessitate an alert, including the precise log patterns or threat thresholds for malicious code detection and logging failures. This requires the customer to configure alert definitions within VCF Operations and vDefend to align with their security policies and operational requirements for Part 4.1 event logging.

Description:
Generate alerts for security events that the Responsible Entity determines necessitates an alert, that includes, as a minimum, each of the following types of events (per Cyber Asset or BES Cyber System capability):

4.2.2. Detected failure of Part 4.1 event logging.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for maintaining the integrity of the virtualized infrastructure and detecting various security anomalies. The platform enforces integrity through Secure Boot, code signing, Trusted Platform Module support, and host attestation, ensuring that hosts boot into a known good configuration. vSphere Lifecycle Manager and Configuration Profiles maintain desired-state configuration for ESX hosts, detecting and remediating drift. VCF Operations monitors the overall security state of the environment and can generate alerts when anomalies are detected. VMware vDefend further enhances security with advanced threat prevention, including real-time malware detection (file-based and fileless), IDS/IPS for network-borne threats, and centralized dashboards for aggregating detection events. These capabilities are critical for identifying and responding to malicious activity and unauthorized changes within the environment.

However, the direct detection and alerting for the *failure of event logging* for Cyber Assets or BES Cyber Systems, as specifically required by Part 4.1, is not explicitly detailed within the described platform capabilities. While VCF Operations can alert on general security anomalies, the customer is responsible for implementing and configuring specific monitoring solutions or integrations to detect and generate alerts for the precise condition of logging failures, ensuring that these events are identified and addressed in accordance with their security policies and operational requirements.

Description:
Generate alerts for security events that the Responsible Entity determines necessitates an alert, that includes, as a minimum, each of the following types of events (per Cyber Asset or BES Cyber System capability):
4.2.1. Detected malicious code from Part 4.1; and
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a comprehensive and centralized alerting framework that directly addresses the requirement to generate alerts for detected malicious code. VCF Operations supports robust alert definitions based on log events from all monitored systems. Administrators can configure symptom definitions to detect specific conditions within ingested logs, including those indicating malicious code detection from underlying cyber assets or BES Cyber System capabilities. These symptoms are are then combined into alert definitions that notify appropriate personnel through various channels.

VCF Operations for Logs specifically enables granular, log-based alerting through configurable symptom definitions, allowing the platform to monitor host systems for specific security conditions and generate alerts when those conditions are detected. The platform's ability to receive fault events from monitored components via REST APIs and process logs forwarded from diverse sources ensures that it can act as the central mechanism for consolidating and alerting on critical security events, including those signifying malicious code detection.

While VMware Live Recovery (VLR) provides event logging with configurable alarms for operational failures across the disaster recovery environment, its primary focus is on disaster recovery operational health and replication error states. VLR's general failure alerting mechanisms can contribute to broader operational monitoring, but the core capability for generating alerts specifically for detected malicious code, particularly through log-based analysis from various cyber assets, is provided by the underlying VCF platform's monitoring stack.

The customer is responsible for ensuring that malicious code detection solutions are deployed on relevant cyber assets, that their detection events are logged and ingested into VCF's monitoring stack, and that the specific alert definitions for these malicious code events are properly configured within VCF Operations.

Description:
Where technically feasible, retain
applicable event logs identified in Part
4.1 for at least the last 90 consecutive
calendar days except under CIP
Exceptional Circumstances.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust, configurable log retention capabilities across its integrated components. VCF Operations for Logs serves as a centralized log management platform, allowing organizations to configure retention periods for log data, with audit events viewable across selectable time ranges determined by these settings. VCF Automation includes its own activity log history retention setting, enabling administrators to specify the number of days to retain logs, with an option to disable automatic deletion for indefinite preservation. At the hypervisor layer, ESX hosts maintain a configurable number of log files through a rolling retention model, which administrators can size to meet specific minimum retention requirements.

All VCF components support syslog forwarding to external log management systems and SIEM platforms, facilitating centralized retention policy enforcement and long-term archival beyond built-in storage.

VMware Live Recovery offers configurable log retention mechanisms for disaster recovery operations, preserving recovery plan history and allowing administrators to configure the retention of exportable history reports. VLR also manages log rotation and compression, with per-component logging levels and rate limit log purge intervals being adjustable. Event logs from VLR can be forwarded via vCenter's syslog to external log management platforms for extended retention.

VMware vDefend retains security event data generated by its features, with Network Detection and Response event and campaign data retained for 12 months, and other security metrics having configurable retention periods.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms for log retention, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing the specific retention policies across all applicable event logs to ensure they are retained for at least 90 consecutive calendar days. This includes configuring the retention settings within VCF Operations for Logs, VCF Automation, ESX hosts, and VMware Live Recovery, as well as establishing and managing external log management or SIEM solutions for centralized aggregation and long-term archival of all relevant logs as required by the control.

Description:
Review a summarization or sampling
of logged events as determined by the
Responsible Entity at intervals no
greater than 15 calendar days to
identify undetected Cyber Security
Incidents.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide comprehensive capabilities for event log generation, analysis, and incident triage. VCF Operations for Logs serves as the primary platform for log analysis, enabling operators to search, filter, aggregate, and identify trends and anomalies across ingested log data. Log-based alerts can be configured through Symptom Definitions for automated notifications. VCF Operations includes a dedicated Troubleshooting Incidents page for managing incidents, tracking resolution, and performing root cause analysis through Diagnostic Findings, which integrates log-based insights.

At the infrastructure layer, vCenter Server provides real-time event monitoring, NSX logs all auditable events, and VCF Automation records detailed audit logs. VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by generating high-fidelity security event logs from its Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, Distributed IDS/IPS, and Advanced Threat Prevention services. Network Detection and Response (NDR) further enriches this by collecting and analyzing security event data for real-time monitoring.

Both VCF and vDefend support native syslog forwarding to external SIEM platforms, ensuring that all relevant log data feeds into established security operations center (SOC) workflows and incident response tooling for comprehensive analysis and triage.

While the platform provides the necessary technical tools for log collection, analysis, and incident identification, the customer is responsible for defining the specific summarization or sampling methodology for logged events. This includes establishing the criteria for what constitutes a reviewable sample and determining the scope of the review. Furthermore, the customer must implement the operational processes to ensure these reviews are conducted by a responsible entity at intervals no greater than 15 calendar days, as the platform does not automate the human act of review or enforce the review frequency.

Description:
Have a method(s) to enforce
authentication of interactive user access,
where technically feasible.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust, centralized authentication service through VCF SSO, which is fundamental for enforcing authentication of interactive user access across the platform. VCF SSO issues security tokens for access to vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and other platform components. It supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, ADFS, and Okta, enabling multifactor authentication and automatic user synchronization.

VCF Automation extends these capabilities by allowing organizations to define shared identity providers and govern project access. VMware Kubernetes Service supports authentication via VCF SSO and external OpenID Connect providers for Kubernetes workloads. NSX Manager integrates with LDAP and external identity management services for administrative access. VMware Live Recovery leverages VCF SSO for authentication of disaster recovery operations.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) implements its own authentication server, supporting local or directory-managed accounts (LDAPS, LDAP, OpenLDAP) for access to security functions, and its Identity Firewall enforces network policy based on authenticated user identity. Solution users authenticate between VCF components using certificates for secure service-to-service communications.

While VCF provides the comprehensive technical enforcement layer for authentication, the organization is responsible for defining the specific authentication policies, configuring the integration with their enterprise identity providers, and managing user accounts and groups. The customer must ensure these capabilities are deployed and configured appropriately within their environment to align with their security posture and governance objectives.

Description:
Identify and inventory all known enabled
default or other generic account types,
either by system, by groups of systems, by
location, or by system type(s).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for managing and inventorying user and service accounts across the platform. VCF SSO serves as the centralized identity store, federating with external identity providers to synchronize user and group information, which offers a consolidated view of who has access. VCF Operations supports importing, editing, exporting, and deleting user accounts, facilitating periodic account reviews. VCF Automation distinguishes between user and service accounts, enabling API-based access with rotating API tokens and offering granular control over visibility through the Limited Service Accounts View right. The vCenter UserDirectory service returns user account information, and VCF Automation supports bulk user management through CSV export/import. These integrated features provide the technical mechanisms necessary to enumerate, manage, and audit user and service accounts.

While VCF delivers the technical foundation for account enumeration and management, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing the identity governance program. This includes establishing policies for identifying and classifying default or generic account types, maintaining a current and authoritative inventory of all authorized accounts, and ensuring the ongoing review and reconciliation of these accounts to meet the control's specific requirements.

Description:
Identify individuals who have authorized
access to shared accounts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive account management capabilities for its infrastructure platform, encompassing user, group, system, and service accounts. VCF SSO acts as the centralized identity service, enabling administrators to manage individual and group accounts through vCenter and integrate with external identity sources such as Active Directory, LDAP, and OIDC. This integration ensures that account lifecycle management can align with existing enterprise processes.

VCF Operations further extends these capabilities with user account creation, editing, deletion, import/export from LDAP, and granular user group and role assignments. Each VCF component, including NSX Manager, ESX hosts (via vCenter brokering), and SDDC Manager, supports account management appropriate to its function, often leveraging role-based access control (RBAC) to enforce the principle of least privilege.

Similarly, within its management plane, VMware vDefend Security Services Platform maintains built-in local user accounts with defined roles and supports directory-managed accounts via external identity providers like Active Directory over LDAP for remote users and groups. These capabilities allow for the explicit definition and management of who has authorized access to accounts within the respective platform's operational boundaries.

While VCF and vDefend provide the technical mechanisms to manage and identify authorized access to accounts within their scope, the broader organizational governance of account management across all enterprise systems remains a customer responsibility.

This includes defining and implementing organizational policies for account lifecycle management, such as provisioning approvals, periodic access reviews, account termination procedures, and temporary account duration limits. These overarching processes and the tooling required for enterprise-wide identity and access management extend beyond the inherent capabilities of VCF and vDefend, necessitating customer-defined procedures to fully address the identification of individuals with authorized access to shared accounts across the entire IT estate.

Description:
Change known default passwords, per
Cyber Asset capability
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) ensures that known default passwords are changed or never established during initial deployment. The VCF Installer Deployment Wizard mandates the specification or auto-generation of unique passwords for every component, including VCF Operations, SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX, and ESX hosts. The deployment process will not proceed without these unique password configurations, thereby preventing any component from running with shared or factory-default credentials.

Post-deployment, SDDC Manager provides centralized password management, enabling administrators to rotate passwords on demand or schedule automatic rotation. During rotation, SDDC Manager generates randomized passwords conforming to component-specific policies and automatically re-establishes relevant component integrations. The SDDC Manager API further supports scheduling custom rotation intervals for automation workflows.

Provider management accounts authenticate using API access tokens that rotate after each use, inherently preventing token reuse. VCF Operations also offers a dedicated Rotate function for system-managed credentials, requiring administrators to rotate these for individual endpoints. VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform Installer similarly requires that default passwords for its admin and audit user accounts be set during deployment, adhering to defined password rules and ensuring default authenticators are replaced at installation time for its management plane.

The platform's inherent design and automated capabilities comprehensively address the requirement to change known default passwords, largely eliminating the need for manual intervention to identify and replace initial default credentials.

Description:
For password-only authentication for interactive user access, either technically or procedurally enforce the following password parameters:
5.5.1. Password length that is, at least, the lesser of eight characters or the maximum length supported by the Cyber Asset; and
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend components rigorously enforce password length requirements for interactive user access, consistently exceeding the minimum of eight characters specified by the control. NSX Manager and NSX Edge, foundational elements across both VCF and vDefend deployments, mandate a minimum password length of 12 characters, with support for up to 128 characters. VCF Operations further strengthens this posture by enforcing a minimum password length of 15 characters. The Security Services Platform (SSP) within vDefend similarly requires a minimum of 12 characters for local user accounts.

These parameters are technically enforced through platform mechanisms, including Linux PAM modules and dedicated policy settings within the respective management interfaces. SDDC Manager centralizes password lifecycle management, generating randomized passwords that conform to these stringent component-specific policies, and vCenter's local account password policy is fully manageable via the VCF API.

The platform's default configurations and configurable options ensure that all interactive user access passwords meet or exceed the required length. While administrators retain the flexibility to adjust these parameters within the platform's supported ranges, the inherent technical enforcement capabilities directly fulfill the control's mandate for password length.

Description:
For password-only authentication for interactive user access, either technically or procedurally enforce the following password parameters:

5.5.2. Minimum password complexity that is the lesser of three or more different types of characters (e.g., uppercase alphabetic, lowercase alphabetic, numeric, nonalphanumeric) or the maximum complexity supported by the Cyber Asset.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide robust technical capabilities for enforcing password complexity, length, and lifespan across their respective components and management interfaces.

VCF components, including NSX Manager, NSX Edge, and VCF Operations, enforce stringent password policies. NSX Manager and NSX Edge leverage Linux PAM modules to require a minimum of 12 characters, including at least one lowercase, one uppercase, one numeric, and one special character, with a total of at least five different character types. NSX Edge further restricts dictionary words, palindromes, and monotonic sequences. VCF Operations mandates a minimum of 15 characters with requirements for uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special characters, managed through its Global Settings.

SDDC Manager centralizes password lifecycle management, enabling on-demand or scheduled rotation of randomized, policy-compliant passwords for VCF components, and provides remediation for password drift. The vCenter Password API also supports service account password resets.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) enforces similar strong policies for local user accounts, requiring a minimum of 12 characters with at least one uppercase, one lowercase, one numeric, and one special character, along with password expiration. NSX Manager and NSX Edge, which manage vDefend services, apply their established complexity rules.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for enforcing password parameters, the customer is responsible for defining the specific password policy values within the configurable ranges, establishing the associated processes for password management (e.g., rotation schedules, account lockout procedures), and ensuring consistent application and monitoring of these policies across their environment. For enterprise-wide authentication, customers must also configure integration with directory services such as Active Directory or OpenLDAP through SSP.

Description:
Where technically feasible, for
password-only authentication for
interactive user access, either
technically or procedurally enforce
password changes or an obligation to
change the password at least once
every 15 calendar months.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for managing authenticators across its components, significantly enhancing security posture. VCF SSO serves as the centralized authentication service, facilitating federation with enterprise identity providers. This integration enables advanced authentication methods such as multifactor authentication (MFA) and token-based credentials, thereby reducing the reliance on password-only authentication for interactive user access.

For local accounts, individual VCF components enforce stringent password complexity and lifecycle policies. NSX Manager, for example, requires passwords of at least 12 characters with diverse character types and offers configurable password expiration, with a default of 90 days and a configurable range extending up to 9,999 days. VCF Operations similarly enforces strong password requirements and configurable account lockout policies.

Service-to-service authentication within VCF leverages certificate-based credentials and rotating API access tokens, eliminating password reliance for these interactions.

While VCF furnishes the technical mechanisms to enforce password expiration, complexity, and rotation within its components, the ultimate responsibility for defining and implementing the specific password change frequency, such as the 15-month interval, rests with the customer. This includes configuring the expiration settings for local accounts and establishing comprehensive organizational policies that govern authenticator lifecycle management across all integrated systems and identity providers.

Description:
Where technically feasible, either:
 Limit the number of
unsuccessful authentication
attempts; or
 Generate alerts after a
threshold of unsuccessful
authentication attempts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms to limit unsuccessful authentication attempts across its management components.

VCF Operations enforces account lockout policies, locking accounts after a configurable number of failed login attempts, with a default threshold of seven attempts. For VCF Operations for Networks, accounts are locked after five consecutive failed attempts, with an initial 15-minute lockout that escalates with subsequent failures, up to a maximum of one day. The super administrator account lockout policy is activated by default and cannot be deactivated.

VCF Automation supports account lockout after a configurable number of invalid login attempts when enabled through password policy system settings, allowing administrators to manually unlock accounts if needed.

NSX Manager also supports configurable lockout periods and maximum authentication failure thresholds via CLI commands.

Administrators are responsible for configuring these lockout settings to align with organizational security policies and for enabling them where required, such as within VCF Automation. While the platform provides the capability to limit unsuccessful authentication attempts, the generation of alerts after a threshold of unsuccessful authentication attempts is not a native capability described within these components.

Description:
One or more processes to identify,
classify, and respond to Cyber Security
Incidents.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical infrastructure to support several phases of the incident response lifecycle. It correlates events from monitored systems, surfacing them as faults ranked by severity from Info to Immediate, and allows for notification rules and vCenter Server alarms to trigger actions for automated detection.

The Troubleshooting Incidents page enables operators to manage incidents, track status, impact, urgency, and priority, and flag root causes. VCF integrates with ServiceNow for structured incident flow into existing ITSM workflows.

For containment, vCenter Server supports isolating affected workloads through VM power state changes and network reconfiguration. Recovery capabilities include vSphere HA for automatic VM restarts, Proactive HA for workload evacuation before failures, and vSphere Fault Tolerance for zero-downtime protection.

VMware Live Recovery provides structured incident handling for disaster recovery scenarios. Its event logging and alarm framework detects data protection incidents such as site connectivity loss or replication failures.

Recovery plans define documented, repeatable response procedures for VM recovery sequences, network reconfiguration, and custom steps, which can be tested non-disruptively. VLR orchestrates automated failover for planned migrations and disaster recovery, with history reports documenting execution outcomes.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with integrated threat detection, investigation, correlation, and remediation within the network security layer.

Its Security Services Platform detects and correlates malicious activity, such as lateral movement and command-and-control traffic, across the environment to surface multi-stage attack campaigns. Network Detection and Response (NDR) aggregates detection data and assigns impact scores to campaigns and events, while Security Intelligence categorizes suspicious traffic by impact type (Critical, High, Medium, Low) for prioritized triage.

For remediation, vDefend Intelligent Assist provides AI-guided workflows to create targeted or comprehensive remediation policies, which are subject to human review and approval, and can immediately contain threats by creating rules in the Distributed Firewall's Emergency section. Quarantine policies automatically isolate affected VMs while preserving forensic access.

While these platforms offer extensive technical capabilities for incident identification, classification, and response, the organization remains responsible for defining and documenting the overarching incident response processes, establishing policies, assigning human accountability, and governing the full incident management lifecycle. This includes organizational preparation, manual intake, and comprehensive recovery phases that extend beyond the automated capabilities of the platforms.

VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical capabilities for defining and evaluating attempts to compromise. VCF Operations supports automated event detection, correlating events from monitored infrastructure components into faults, which are then ranked by severity. It enables administrators to define custom alerts based on metric and log-based symptoms, incorporating Smart Early Warning conditions and log event patterns to flag suspicious activity.

NSX integrates with external threat intelligence feeds to identify and block known malicious IP addresses and utilizes virtual Deep Packet Inspection (vDPI) for signature-based packet payload analysis. Log forwarding from vCenter, NSX, and ESX hosts to external SIEM platforms further supports the operationalization of Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) and external correlation.

VMware vDefend significantly enhances these capabilities through its Security Intelligence, which detects and prioritizes suspicious traffic events by impact type and assigns impact scores to campaigns, facilitating incident triage. It offers full IDS/IPS functionality, including the ability to import custom and third-party threat intelligence signatures, and provides custom signature bundle management for tailored IOC definitions.

NSX Advanced Threat Prevention combines IDS/IPS with network sandboxing and Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) through Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlation engines, providing comprehensive mechanisms to define and evaluate potential cybersecurity events.

While the platform delivers the technical tools for defining criteria and evaluating compromise attempts, the organization is responsible for establishing the formal criteria, developing the overarching incident management and categorization processes, and governing the sourcing, vetting, and curation of Indicators of Compromise.

Description:
To determine if an identified
Cyber Security Incident is:
• A Reportable Cyber
Security Incident; or
• An attempt to
compromise, as
determined by applying
the criteria from Part
1.2.1, one or more
systems identified in the
“Applicable Systems”
column for this Part; and
Response:
VMware vDefend's Security Intelligence categorizes detected suspicious traffic events by impact type, including Critical, High, Medium, and Low, enabling security teams to prioritize incident triage based on severity. Network Detection and Response (NDR) assigns impact scores to campaigns, with higher scores indicating higher priority for triage and response, and classifies detection events into impact levels, including Informational.

IDS/IPS and Malware Prevention alerts provide additional contextual signals that contribute to incident classification. VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports custom alert definitions that combine metric-based and log-based symptoms, allowing administrators to define conditions that flag suspicious activity. These alert definitions incorporate Smart Early Warning conditions and log event patterns from monitored systems. Problem alerts are logged to syslog, and outbound notification plug-ins (SNMP Trap, webhook, email) forward these alerts to external security tools for correlation and triage. VCF Operations for Logs enables log-based symptom definitions that match patterns across aggregated log data.

NSX integrates with threat intelligence feeds through its Malicious IP Feed capability, downloading lists of known malicious IP addresses from the NTICS cloud service for use in network policy. NSX also supports vDPI (virtual Deep Packet Inspection), matching packet payloads against defined signature patterns for identification and enforcement. vDefend extends these capabilities with full IDS/IPS functionality, including support for importing custom and third-party threat intelligence signatures, and its custom signature bundle management allows organizations to upload, validate, and manage tailored Indicators of Compromise (IOC) definitions. NSX Advanced Threat Prevention combines IDS/IPS with network sandboxing and Network Traffic Analysis (NTA), aggregated through Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlation engines.

Log forwarding across VCF components, including vCenter, NSX, and ESX hosts, supports IOC operationalization by sending syslog data to external SIEM platforms for matching and correlation. NSX audit logs include structured syslog fields that identify security-relevant events, and remote logging can be filtered by severity level and message categories.

While the platform provides robust technical capabilities for detecting, classifying, and prioritizing potential cybersecurity events, the formal determination of whether an identified incident constitutes a "Reportable Cyber Security Incident" or an "attempt to compromise" based on specific organizational criteria (e.g., from Part 1.2.1) remains an organizational responsibility. The broader incident management and categorization process, including the governance of an IOC program—sourcing, vetting, and curating IOCs—is governed by customer-defined policies and procedures outside the scope of the platform.
