Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall have one or more documented recovery plan(s) that collectively include each of the
applicable requirement parts in CIP-009-6 Table R1 – Recovery Plan Specifications. [Violation Risk Factor: Medium] [Time
Horizon: Long Term Planning].
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a resilient infrastructure foundation for recovery planning through integrated business continuity and disaster recovery capabilities.

vSphere High Availability automatically restarts virtual machines upon host failure, ensuring workload resilience.

vSAN stretched clusters extend logical clusters across availability zones, maintaining operations during zone failures, complemented by vSAN Data Protection for snapshot-based protection and replication.

The platform mandates regular backup schedules and retention policies for all management components, including SDDC Manager, vCenter, and NSX, with specific recommendations for frequency and consistency.

VMware HCX facilitates workload mobility and replication between sites, supporting various migration scenarios from bulk transfers to zero-downtime live migrations.

VCF Operations monitors infrastructure health, provides alerts, and offers capacity planning tools to proactively manage resource availability.

VMware Live Recovery is purpose-built to define, manage, and execute contingency plans for virtualized infrastructure.

It enables the creation of recovery plans that specify the sequence, priority, and configuration for failover operations, effectively serving as executable BC/DR playbooks. Protection groups organize virtual machines into logical units for comprehensive planning. The platform supports non-disruptive testing to validate procedures without impacting production, and its state management and status monitoring provide visibility into readiness.

VMware Live Recovery offers both planned migration and disaster recovery modes, leveraging vSphere Replication for host-based replication with granular RPO settings and integrating with array-based replication for storage-level protection. It supports bidirectional protection and automates network reconfiguration during recovery with the DR IP Customizer tool.

VMware vDefend further contributes by ensuring that security posture, including distributed and gateway firewall policies, is maintained during disaster recovery events and workload migrations, preventing security gaps as workloads fail over.

While the platform provides robust technical capabilities and tools for defining, testing, and executing recovery plans, the responsible entity must still define the specific content of these documented recovery plans, ensuring they collectively include all applicable requirement parts from CIP-009-6 Table R1.

The organization is accountable for establishing the broader Business Continuity Management System (BCMS), including conducting impact analyses, developing comprehensive COOP and BC/DR playbooks, and implementing organizational governance processes, which are outside the scope of the technical platform.

Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall implement its documented recovery plan(s) to collectively include each of the applicable
requirement parts in CIP-009-6 Table R2 – Recovery Plan Implementation and Testing. [Violation Risk Factor: Lower] [Time
Horizon: Operations Planning and Real-time Operations.]
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust foundation for implementing recovery plans through integrated business continuity and disaster recovery capabilities. vSphere High Availability (HA) automatically restarts virtual machines upon host failure, ensuring workload resilience, while vSAN stretched clusters extend logical storage across availability zones for continuous operation during zone failures. vSAN Data Protection offers snapshot-based protection with configurable retention and replication.

VCF mandates regular backup schedules and retention policies for all management components, including SDDC Manager, vCenter, and NSX, with features like Changed Block Tracking (CBT) optimizing backup efficiency.

VMware HCX facilitates workload mobility and replication across sites, supporting various migration scenarios. VCF Operations monitors infrastructure health, provides alerts, and offers capacity planning tools to proactively manage availability risks.

VMware Live Recovery is purpose-built for contingency planning, enabling the creation of executable recovery plans that define the sequence, priority, and configuration for failover operations, organized into protection groups. It supports non-disruptive testing to validate procedures without impacting production, offers state management, and provides visibility into readiness for both planned migrations and disaster recovery.

vSphere Replication delivers host-based replication with RPOs down to 60 seconds, complemented by array-based replication integration. Live Recovery supports bidirectional protection, dynamic resource updates, and automates network reconfiguration during recovery with the DR IP Customizer, integrating with DRS and DPM for resource optimization.

VMware vDefend further contributes by maintaining security posture, including distributed and gateway firewall policies, during disaster recovery events and workload migrations via a Global Manager deployment, ensuring security controls remain active throughout contingency plan activation.

The responsible entity must define, document, and formally approve its comprehensive recovery plan(s), including all applicable requirements from CIP-009-6 Table R2. This includes establishing the overarching Business Continuity Management System (BCMS), conducting impact analyses, and defining organizational governance processes, human accountability, and specific procedures for the execution and testing of these recovery plans.

Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall maintain each of its recovery plan(s) in accordance with each of the applicable requirement parts
in CIP-009-6 Table R3 – Recovery Plan Review, Update and Communication. [Violation Risk Factor: Lower] [Time Horizon:
Operations Assessment].
Response:
VMware Live Recovery provides the core capabilities for defining, testing, and managing executable recovery plans, which serve as comprehensive BC/DR playbooks. These plans organize virtual machines into protection groups and precisely define the sequence, priority, and configuration for failover operations. The platform supports non-disruptive testing, allowing organizations to validate their contingency procedures without impacting production workloads, and offers robust plan state management and status monitoring to ensure readiness. It integrates host-based replication with configurable Recovery Point Objectives (RPO) and supports array-based replication, bidirectional protection, dynamic resource mapping updates, and automated network reconfiguration during recovery.

VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure for business continuity and disaster recovery, including vSphere High Availability for automatic VM restarts, vSAN stretched clusters for resilience across availability zones, and vSAN Data Protection for snapshot-based data protection. VCF mandates regular backup schedules and retention policies for all management components, ensuring the recoverability of the SDDC infrastructure itself.

VMware HCX facilitates workload mobility, migration, and replication between sites, supporting various data movement scenarios.

VCF Operations monitors infrastructure health, provides alerts, and offers capacity planning tools to proactively manage resource availability.

VMware vDefend ensures the continuity of security posture during disaster recovery events and workload migrations by maintaining distributed firewall and gateway firewall policies through a Global Manager deployment, preventing security gaps when contingency plans are activated.

While the platform provides robust technical tools for defining, testing, and executing recovery plans, the responsible entity must establish and enforce the overarching governance framework. This includes defining formal policies for the periodic review, update, and communication of these recovery plans, as well as assigning human accountability for these processes. The organization is responsible for integrating these technical capabilities into a comprehensive business continuity management system, including impact analyses and organizational governance.

Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall implement one or more documented process(es) that
collectively include each of the applicable requirement parts in CIP-010-3 Table R1 –
Configuration Change Management. [Violation Risk Factor: Medium] [Time Horizon:
Operations Planning].
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for configuration management, including Configuration Templates to define desired states, integration with source control for versioning, auditing, and change control, and automated testing and validation of changes. Configuration Drifts Templates track deviations, while policies enforce configuration standards. vSphere Lifecycle Manager ensures consistency through image-based lifecycle management and Configuration Profiles. VCF Automation extends these capabilities through integration with external tools for automated workload configuration enforcement. VMware Live Recovery offers specific configuration management for disaster recovery operations, enabling export and import of DR configuration data for versioning and backup, structured modification of network configurations via the DR IP Customizer, and CLI tools for managing appliance properties. VMware vDefend contributes robust configuration management for network security policies, featuring draft configuration workflows for review and validation before publication, a federated architecture for centralized policy management across multiple sites, and deployment validation with configuration synchronization metrics. Security Intelligence further supports configuration management by maintaining application context in a CMDB for segmentation posture assessment and planning.

While the platform provides extensive technical capabilities to implement and manage configuration changes, the control explicitly requires the implementation of one or more *documented processes*. The customer is responsible for defining, documenting, and enforcing these formal configuration change management processes, establishing roles and responsibilities, and integrating these technical capabilities into an overarching organizational policy framework to ensure compliance with the documented requirements.

VMware Cloud Foundation develops, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations consistent with industry-accepted hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline is a consolidated document that addresses frequently requested compliance standards, regulations, and frameworks, providing tailored security configurations without affecting VCF's design objectives. VCF offers detailed hardening guidance for major components such as vCenter and NSX, covering aspects like installation, access, authentication, roles, permissions, auditing, and logging. Broadcom further provides DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) and STIG Readiness Guides for VCF components, offering prescriptive baseline configurations for operating systems, applications, and network settings. VCF Operations includes compliance scanning capabilities to evaluate infrastructure against these baselines, ensuring consistent configuration across the SDDC, and the VCF 9 Security Baseline provides a consolidated, out-of-the-box benchmark.

VMware Live Recovery provides configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including TLS configuration parameters and documented network encryption requirements for replication traffic.

VMware vDefend contributes to secure baseline configurations within its network security domain through structured configuration management capabilities, predefined security profiles, and configuration integrity controls. This includes draft configuration management with locking and comment requirements for Distributed Firewall policies, predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles for Gateway Firewall, and pre-deployment validation of configuration changes.

The Security Services Platform also supports backup and restore of configuration data and offers a Security Assessment capability to evaluate workload configurations against expected security standards, with configurable modes to align with organizational baseline requirements.

While the platform provides extensive technical capabilities, predefined secure baselines, and hardening guidance for its components, the organization is ultimately responsible for formally developing, documenting, and approving its comprehensive baseline configuration. This includes defining specific versions for all operating systems and applications, detailing any custom software installed within customer workloads, and explicitly listing all logical network accessible ports and applied security patches as part of their overall configuration management program. The customer must also establish the policies and processes for baseline adherence, review, and deviation management.

Description:
Develop a baseline configuration, individually or by group, which shall include the following items:
1.1.1. Operating system(s) (including version) or firmware where no independent operating system exists;
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) develops, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations for its operating systems and firmware, aligning with industry-accepted hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline is a comprehensive document that consolidates security fundamentals and addresses frequently requested compliance standards and frameworks. This includes detailed hardening guidance for major components such as vCenter Server and NSX, covering installation security, access controls, roles, permissions, auditing, and logging.

Broadcom further provides DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) and STIG Readiness Guides for VCF components, offering prescriptive guidance for secure configurations.

VCF Operations incorporates compliance scanning capabilities to evaluate infrastructure against these established baselines, ensuring consistent configuration across the Software-Defined Data Center (SDDC) components. VMware Live Recovery contributes by providing configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including TLS protocol versions, and documents network encryption requirements. VMware vDefend further contributes within its network security domain through structured configuration management, predefined security profiles for firewalls (e.g., TLS Decryption Action Profiles), and configuration integrity controls, all validated through pre-deployment checks and supported by backup/restore capabilities. The Security Services Platform's Security Assessment capability allows for evaluation against configurable baseline requirements.

While VCF provides the developed and documented baseline configurations for its components, customers are responsible for adopting these baselines, implementing them within their specific operational environment, and establishing processes to ensure continuous adherence. This includes defining organizational policies for baseline enforcement, tailoring configurations as necessary to meet unique business requirements while maintaining security posture, and integrating the provided compliance scanning and assessment tools into their ongoing security operations.

Description:
Develop a baseline configuration, individually or by group, which shall include the following items:

1.1.2. Any commercially available or open-source application software (including version) intentionally installed;
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation develops, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations for its core components, including vCenter, NSX, and other SDDC elements. This includes detailing application versions and adhering to industry-accepted hardening standards, as outlined in the VCF Security Baseline document, component-specific hardening guides, and DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs). VCF Operations provides compliance scanning capabilities to evaluate the infrastructure against these established baselines, ensuring consistent configuration across SDDC components.

VMware Live Recovery establishes configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including TLS configurations and documented network encryption requirements.

VMware vDefend contributes by maintaining secure defaults and configuration controls within its network security domain, offering structured configuration management for distributed and gateway firewalls, and validating configuration changes before deployment. Additionally, vDefend's Security Services Platform includes a Security Assessment capability that evaluates the security posture of workloads and applications against configurable organizational baseline requirements, recording detected issues with CVSS scores and CVE identifiers for tracking.

While the platform components establish and maintain their own secure baselines, the comprehensive development of baseline configurations for *all* commercially available or open-source application software intentionally installed, particularly those deployed within guest virtual machines, requires customer-defined policies, processes, and accountability. The customer is responsible for identifying, documenting, and maintaining baselines for applications beyond the core VCF platform components, leveraging vDefend's assessment capabilities to verify adherence to those baselines.

Description:
Develop a baseline configuration, individually or by group, which shall include the following items:

1.1.3. Any custom software installed;
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations for its integrated components, adhering to industry-accepted hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline, component-specific hardening guides, and DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) provide comprehensive guidance for securing the platform. VCF Operations includes compliance scanning capabilities to evaluate infrastructure against these baselines, ensuring consistent configuration across SDDC components.

VMware Live Site Recovery (VLR) provides configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including TLS configuration and network encryption requirements. VMware vDefend further contributes by offering structured configuration management, predefined security profiles, and configuration integrity controls within its network security domain. This includes draft configuration management with locking for Distributed Firewall, predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles for Gateway Firewall, and pre-deployment validation and backup/restore capabilities within the Security Services Platform.

While VCF provides a robust, baseline-managed foundation for the underlying infrastructure and its integrated services, the specific requirement to include "any custom software installed" in a baseline configuration necessitates customer action. The platform manages the baseline of its own components. However, the identification, documentation, and establishment of baseline configurations for custom software deployed by the customer within virtual machines or on top of the VCF platform, including any associated hardening, remains the customer's responsibility.

Description:
Develop a baseline configuration, individually or by group, which shall include the following items:

1.1.4. Any logical network accessible ports; and
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust framework for developing, documenting, and maintaining secure baseline configurations consistent with industry-accepted hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline is a consolidated document that addresses frequently requested compliance standards, regulations, and frameworks, designed to tailor security configurations without affecting VCF's design objectives.

VCF offers specific hardening guidance for major components, including vCenter and NSX, and Broadcom provides DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) and STIG Readiness Guides for VCF components. VCF Operations includes compliance scanning capabilities that evaluate infrastructure against these baseline configurations, ensuring consistency across SDDC components.

VMware vDefend further contributes to secure baseline configurations within its network security domain through structured configuration management capabilities, predefined security profiles, and configuration integrity controls.

vDefend Distributed Firewall supports draft configuration management with locking and comment requirements, while vDefend Gateway Firewall provides predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles as vendor-defined baseline configurations. The Security Services Platform validates configuration changes pre-deployment and supports backup and restore of configuration data. Its Security Assessment capability evaluates workload security posture against configurable modes aligned to organizational baseline requirements.

VMware Live Recovery provides configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including adjustable TLS configurations and documented network encryption requirements for replication traffic.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical tools, predefined baselines, hardening guidance, and compliance scanning capabilities, the customer is ultimately responsible for defining, adopting, and actively managing their specific organizational baseline configuration for all logical network accessible ports. This includes implementing the recommended configurations, establishing policies for their use, and responding to any deviations identified through compliance assessments.

Description:
Develop a baseline configuration, individually or by group, which shall include the following items:

1.1.5. Any security patches applied.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation develops, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations consistent with industry-accepted hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline is a consolidated document that integrates security fundamentals to address compliance requirements, providing a validated framework for tailoring security configurations without impacting design objectives.

This baseline, along with component-specific hardening guides and Broadcom-provided DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) and STIG Readiness Guides, explicitly defines the required security patch levels for VCF components.

VCF Operations further supports this by offering compliance scanning capabilities that evaluate the infrastructure against these established baselines, ensuring consistent configuration and adherence to specified patch levels across all SDDC components. The introduction of the VCF 9 Security Baseline consolidates these benchmarks, streamlining the process of maintaining a secure and patched environment.

Within this broader VCF framework, VMware Live Recovery provides configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including TLS configuration parameters, and documents network encryption requirements, contributing to the secure posture of disaster recovery components.

VMware vDefend enhances secure baseline configurations within the network security domain through structured configuration management, predefined security profiles, and integrity controls. Its Distributed Firewall supports draft configuration management with locking and comment requirements, while the Gateway Firewall offers predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles that serve as vendor-defined baseline configurations.

The Security Services Platform validates configuration changes pre-deployment to prevent drift and offers security assessment capabilities that evaluate workload posture against configurable baseline requirements, including tracking issues with CVSS scores and CVE identifiers. These capabilities collectively ensure that security patches and configurations are defined, documented, and maintained as integral parts of the overall secure baseline for the VCF environment.

Description: Authorize and document changes that deviate from the existing baseline configuration.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for detecting and documenting deviations from established baseline configurations. VCF Operations monitors infrastructure objects, generating configuration drift notifications that detail specific changes, their timing, and comparison to desired states. Compliance scoring helps prioritize these deviations, and custom compliance benchmarks can be defined. Integrating VCF Operations configuration drift templates with a source control repository supports change control by allowing reviewed and approved commits to serve as a documented approval trail for accepting deviations.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) generates specific events when replication configurations change, such as the VmReplicationConfigurationChangedEvent, enabling administrators to configure custom alarms for notification. VLR also provides replication reports and diagnostics that capture the current configuration state, supporting periodic reviews against baselines.

VMware vDefend enhances this by providing configuration validation, anomaly detection, and assessment capabilities.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) deployment wizard conducts pre-implementation validations to identify potential deviations before they take effect, and SSP Rule Analysis identifies anomalies like duplicate or conflicting firewall rules. Security Intelligence offers assessment and monitoring tools to evaluate security configurations against baselines, tracking inter-environment policy status and displaying change-detected indicators for group membership or policy scope modifications.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical tools for detecting and documenting configuration deviations, the formal workflow for assessing risk, authorizing, and denying specific deviations requires organizational processes and governance. VCF Automation provides approval policies for new infrastructure deployments and modifications, but these do not directly address the review and approval of *detected configuration drift*.

The decision to remediate a detected deviation or accept it as an approved exception is made by the administrator, often outside of any formal VCF workflow. The effective implementation of a documented approval trail for deviations, particularly through source control integration, depends on the organization establishing and enforcing the necessary integration and review processes.

Description: For a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration, update the baseline configuration as necessary within 30 calendar days of completing the change.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for managing and updating baseline configurations. Its integrated lifecycle management orchestrates updates across all platform components, ensuring infrastructure remains current. VCF includes mechanisms to review and update baselines during system component installations and upgrades. For instance, vSAN system baselines recommend upgrades and critical patches to maintain supported hardware states. Post-upgrade, VCF automatically performs reconciliation of configuration drift fixes within approximately 15 minutes. Furthermore, VCF Operations offers compliance scanning tools that continuously evaluate infrastructure against defined baselines, such as the VCF 9 Security Baseline, to identify configuration drift.

VMware vDefend enhances these capabilities by contributing to baseline configuration review and update processes through its Security Intelligence Policy Recommendations. vDefend analyzes observed traffic to generate and refresh firewall rule recommendations, which can be rerun to detect changes and support periodic review cycles. The Review Recommendations pane facilitates the examination and update of segmentation policy baselines.

While VCF and vDefend provide robust technical tools for detecting deviations and facilitating baseline updates, the ultimate responsibility for defining and enforcing the organizational process to update baselines "as necessary" within 30 calendar days of a change rests with the customer. Administrators must actively review changes in upgraded alert definitions, symptom definitions, recommendations, and views to determine whether to retain modified versions or adopt upgraded versions. Similarly, vDefend's recommendations require review, modification, and publication by security teams. The platform provides the means to identify drift and apply updates, but the governance, decision-making, and procedural enforcement of the 30-day update window for all relevant baseline deviations are customer operational requirements.

Description:
For a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration:
1.4.1. Prior to the change, determine required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 that could be impacted by the change;
1.4.2. Following the change, verify that required cyber security controls determined in 1.4.1 are not adversely affected; and
1.4.3. Document the results of the verification.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for detecting and documenting deviations from standardized configurations. VCF Operations monitors infrastructure objects against compliance benchmarks and configuration baselines, generating configuration drift notifications that detail what changed, when, and how the current state compares to desired values. Compliance scoring helps prioritize deviations. Integrating VCF Operations configuration drift templates with a source control repository can support change control through reviewed and approved commits, providing a documented approval trail. VCF Automation enforces review steps for infrastructure deployment changes.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) generates specific events, such as VmReplicationConfigurationChangedEvent, when replication configurations change, allowing administrators to configure custom alarms for notification. VLR also provides replication reports and diagnostics that capture the current configuration state, supporting periodic reviews.

VMware vDefend contributes through configuration validation, anomaly detection, and assessment. The Security Services Platform (SSP) deployment wizard conducts pre-implementation validations, ensuring proposed configurations are correct and compatible. SSP Rule Analysis identifies anomalies in firewall rule sets, such as duplicate or shadowed rules, which may represent unintended deviations. Security Intelligence offers assessment and monitoring tools, including strict and relaxed evaluation modes, to gauge security posture against baselines and track inter-environment policy configuration status. It displays change-detected indicators for group membership or policy scope changes, prompting review. The segmentation planning workflow supports a staged approach to configuration changes, allowing administrators to review policy recommendations based on observed flows before enforcement.

While the platform provides strong deviation detection, documentation, and assessment capabilities, the formal workflow for determining required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 that could be impacted by a change, conducting a comprehensive risk assessment, and the subsequent formal approval or denial process for detected configuration drift requires organizational governance. The decision to remediate a detected deviation or accept it as an approved exception is made by the administrator outside of any formal VCF workflow for drift. Customers must establish the necessary policies, processes, and human accountability to perform the required impact analysis prior to changes, verify that controls are not adversely affected following changes, and formally document the results of this verification and any associated approvals or exceptions.

Description:
For a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration:
1.4.1. Prior to the change, determine required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 that could be impacted by the change;
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for defining, tailoring, and evaluating security baselines, which are fundamental to understanding the existing security posture. The VCF 9 Security Baseline serves as a consolidated benchmark for assessing both default and customized configurations. VCF Operations and Automation extend this with hierarchical policy systems, enabling administrators to define, scope, and manage security policies, analysis settings, and compliance thresholds across various environments. This structured approach ensures that security configurations are consistently applied and monitored. VMware vDefend further enhances these capabilities by offering extensive mechanisms for tailoring network security baselines, organizing policies into logical categories, and supporting federated policy management across multiple sites. Its segmentation planning and recommendation engine allows for the analysis of proposed network changes, enabling administrators to review suggested groups and rules before publishing them as policy, thereby facilitating a structured approach to pre-change impact assessment for network segmentation.

While the platform provides the essential technical foundation, policy management, and analytical tools necessary to understand and manage security controls, the comprehensive determination of all required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 that could be impacted by an arbitrary configuration change necessitates a defined customer process. This process requires human analysis and interpretation of the platform's data and capabilities to fully assess the implications of a proposed change across the entire scope of CIP-005 and CIP-007 requirements.

Description:
For a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration:
 
1.4.2. Following the change, verify that required cyber security controls determined in 1.4.1 are not adversely affected; and
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust framework for managing and validating infrastructure changes, which inherently reduces the risk of adverse effects on cybersecurity controls.

Its lifecycle management employs a draft-and-validate workflow for host software, drivers, firmware, and add-on components, ensuring changes are complete, correct, and free from drift before commitment.

VCF Automation supports pre-production testing for workload deployments, validating templates for viability prior to release.

Configuration Drifts, integrated with source control, enable continuous integration and development workflows for automated testing and validation of configuration changes.

All VCF components generate comprehensive audit logs, recording configuration changes, the initiating user, and their outcomes, providing essential evidence for post-change review.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) offers built-in testing capabilities for disaster recovery configurations.

Recovery plan testing in Live Site Recovery allows for non-production validation of entire recovery workflows, and administrators can run additional tests after reprotect operations to confirm the validity of new configurations.

VMware vSphere Replication also includes automated and on-demand testing for replication mappings, validating their functionality before reliance for actual recovery.

VMware vDefend contributes through its draft configuration management system for Distributed Firewall policies, allowing proposed changes to be composed, reviewed, and compared against current configurations before publishing.

The Security Services Platform conducts thorough validations before implementing configuration changes and generates pre-check verification reports for upgrades, documenting system readiness.

Security Intelligence monitors for changes to input entities, alerting administrators when the basis for security recommendations may have shifted.

These capabilities collectively provide mechanisms for pre-validation, controlled deployment, and comprehensive logging of changes, which are critical inputs to ensuring cybersecurity controls remain effective.

While these platforms provide extensive tools for validating changes and documenting their implementation, the ultimate responsibility for defining and executing a formal post-change verification process to ensure that all required cybersecurity controls, as determined in 1.4.1, are not adversely affected remains with the customer. This includes establishing specific test procedures, defining acceptable control states, and performing the necessary assessments to confirm the continued efficacy of the broader cybersecurity posture after a change has been deployed.

Description:
For a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration:

1.4.3. Document the results of the verification.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for detecting and documenting deviations from established configuration baselines. VCF Operations monitors infrastructure objects against compliance benchmarks, generating configuration drift notifications that detail what changed, when, and how the current state compares to desired values. The Configuration Drifts view serves as a direct record of these deviations. Custom compliance benchmarks can be defined and applied across instances.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) generates specific events, such as VmReplicationConfigurationChangedEvent, when replication configurations are modified, allowing administrators to configure alarms for notification. VLR also provides replication reports and diagnostics that capture the current configuration state, supporting reviews against baselines. VMware vDefend contributes by providing configuration validation, anomaly detection, and assessment capabilities. The Security Services Platform (SSP) deployment wizard performs pre-implementation validations to prevent deviations, while SSP Rule Analysis identifies anomalies like duplicate or conflicting firewall rules. Security Intelligence offers assessment and monitoring tools to evaluate security configurations against baselines, tracking inter-environment policy status and displaying indicators for changes in group membership or policy scope.

While these platforms provide comprehensive detection and initial documentation of deviations, the formal process of verifying a detected deviation, assessing its associated risk, and documenting the specific results of that verification—such as an approved exception or a remediation decision—requires the implementation of organizational policies and governance workflows. VCF's integration with a source control repository can facilitate a documented approval trail for accepting deviations, but this relies on the organization establishing the integration and review process. The ultimate decision to remediate a detected deviation or accept it as an approved exception is made by the administrator, often outside of any formal platform workflow.

Description:
Where technically feasible, for each change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration:
1.5.1. Prior to implementing any change in the production environment, test the changes in a test environment or test the changes in a production environment where the test is performed in a manner that minimizes adverse effects, that models the baseline configuration to ensure that required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 are not adversely affected; and
1.5.2. Document the results of the testing and, if a test environment was used, the differences between the test environment and the production environment, including a description of the measures used to account for any differences in operation between the test and production environments
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for detecting and documenting deviations from established configuration baselines.

VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure objects against compliance benchmarks, generating configuration drift notifications that detail specific changes, their timing, and the comparison to desired values. This includes compliance scoring to prioritize deviations.

VCF Automation incorporates approval policies that enforce a review step prior to implementing new infrastructure deployments or modifications.

VMware vDefend further enhances this by providing pre-implementation validation through the Security Services Platform (SSP) deployment wizard, which checks proposed configurations for correctness and compatibility before they are applied. Security Intelligence within vDefend offers comprehensive assessment and monitoring tools to evaluate security configurations against desired baselines, identify deviations, and track inter-environment policy status. Its segmentation planning workflow supports a staged approach to configuration changes, allowing for permissive mode deployment, traffic analysis, and policy recommendations before enforcement, effectively serving as a pre-implementation assessment.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) contributes by generating specific events for replication configuration changes and providing reports that capture the current state, forming a technical foundation for identifying deviations in disaster recovery configurations.

While the platform offers strong capabilities for detecting configuration drift, validating changes, and assessing security posture against baselines, the comprehensive testing of changes in a dedicated test environment that precisely models the production baseline, specifically to ensure that required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 are not adversely affected, requires organizational processes and human oversight.

The formal documentation of specific test results, including detailed differences between test and production environments and the measures used to account for operational variances, also remains a customer responsibility.

Organizations must establish governance for risk assessment, formal approval workflows for accepting or remediating deviations, and integrate VCF's deviation detection with source control repositories for a complete change control process.

Description:
Where technically feasible, for each change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration:
1.5.1. Prior to implementing any change in the production environment, test the changes in a test environment or test the changes in a production environment where the test is performed in a manner that minimizes adverse effects, that models the baseline configuration to ensure that required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 are not adversely affected; and
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for validating and testing changes before their deployment into a production environment. Its lifecycle management employs a draft-and-validate workflow for infrastructure changes, including host software, drivers, firmware, and add-on components, ensuring completeness, correctness, and alignment with the desired state. Cloud templates can be tested for deployment viability, verifying connections and project associations prior to release. VCF Operations, through its Configuration Drifts capability, integrates with source control repositories to support continuous integration and continuous development (CI/CD) workflows, enabling automated testing and validation of configuration changes. The platform's audit logging records all configuration changes, the initiating user, and their outcomes, supporting comprehensive change documentation.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further contributes with built-in testing functionalities for disaster recovery configurations. VMware Live Site Recovery allows administrators to run non-disruptive tests of recovery plans, executing all steps without affecting production workloads at either the protected or recovery site, thereby providing a non-production validation of the entire recovery workflow. VMware vSphere Replication offers enhanced replication mapping tests, including quick, full, and automated options, to validate replication configurations before they are relied upon for actual recovery events. These test operations are exposed via REST APIs, facilitating integration into automated validation pipelines.

VMware vDefend provides a structured draft configuration management system for firewall policy changes. Administrators can compose, review, and validate proposed changes in a draft state, comparing them against the current published configuration to understand their exact impact before enforcement. The draft system includes optional locking and comment requirements, enforcing a review step prior to publishing.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) deployment wizard conducts thorough validations to ensure configuration correctness and compatibility before implementation, and for upgrades, it generates pre-check verification reports to document system readiness.

vDefend also supports a test-before-deploy pattern for IDS/IPS signature management, recommending offline editing and re-uploading of custom bundles.

While the platform provides extensive technical tools and workflows for pre-production validation and testing, the establishment of formal, dedicated non-production environments for all types of changes, the definition of comprehensive test procedures, and the explicit verification that changes do not adversely affect required cyber security controls in CIP-005 and CIP-007 remain organizational responsibilities. The platform facilitates these activities but does not fully automate or provide the entire testing infrastructure or the policy-level assurance required by the control.

Description:
Where technically feasible, for each change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration:

1.5.2. Document the results of the testing and, if a test environment was used, the differences between the test environment and the production environment, including a description of the measures used to account for any differences in operation between the test and production environments
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a draft-and-validate workflow for infrastructure changes, ensuring completeness and correctness before committing to a desired state. Cloud templates can be validated for deployment viability, and configuration drift capabilities support integration with source control for automated testing and validation. Audit logging across VCF components records configuration changes, the initiating user, and outcomes.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) offers built-in testing for disaster recovery configurations. Recovery plans can be tested without affecting production, and vSphere Replication provides quick, full, and automated replication mapping tests. These test operations are exposed via REST APIs for integration into automated validation workflows.

VMware vDefend supports a draft-based change workflow for firewall policies, allowing administrators to compose, review, and validate proposed changes before publishing them to the active configuration. Drafts can be exported for offline review and comparison against the current published configuration.

The Security Services Platform includes pre-deployment validations and generates pre-check verification reports for upgrades, documenting system readiness. Security Intelligence monitors changes to input entities, alerting administrators when the basis for recommendations shifts.

While these platforms provide robust tools for testing and documenting changes, the establishment of formal non-production environments, comprehensive test procedures, and the explicit documentation of differences between test and production environments, including measures to account for operational variances, remain organizational responsibilities.

The customer is accountable for defining and implementing the overarching change management process, including the formal comparison of environments and the articulation of compensating controls for any identified differences.

Description:
Prior to a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration associated with baseline items in Parts 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.5, and when the method to do so is available to the Responsible Entity from the software source:
1.6.1. Verify the identity of the software source; and
1.6.2. Verify the integrity of the software obtained from the software source.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for managing and controlling configuration changes across the infrastructure. VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure objects against compliance benchmarks and configuration baselines, generating notifications for any detected configuration drift. VCF Automation implements approval policies that enforce a mandatory review step before infrastructure deployment changes can take effect, establishing a critical gate for all modifications. VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) deployment wizard conducts validations prior to implementing configuration changes, ensuring proposed configurations are correct and compatible with the existing environment. Security Intelligence further supports a staged approach to configuration changes through its segmentation planning workflow, allowing for assessment and review of proposed policies before enforcement. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) also contributes by generating specific events when replication configurations change, enabling administrators to configure alarms for deviations from established baselines and providing reports for periodic reviews. These capabilities collectively establish a comprehensive framework for detecting, reviewing, and gating configuration changes within the environment.

While the platform provides the necessary control points and workflows for managing configuration changes and detecting deviations, the explicit verification of the software source's identity and the integrity of the software obtained from that source, as required prior to a change, remains an organizational responsibility. The platform does not natively perform cryptographic verification of software vendor identity or validate the integrity of software binaries (e.g., via checksums or digital signatures) before they are introduced or used to implement configuration deviations. Organizations must establish and integrate their own processes and tools to perform these specific software source and integrity checks within the change management workflows facilitated by VCF, VLR, and vDefend.

Description:
Prior to a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration associated with baseline items in Parts 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.5, and when the method to do so is available to the Responsible Entity from the software source:
1.6.1. Verify the identity of the software source; and
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide robust, integrated capabilities for managing, tailoring, and enforcing security baselines and configuration changes across the entire software-defined data center.

VCF supports the creation and application of security baselines, including the VCF 9 Security Baseline and DISA STIGs, which can be customized to specific operational environments and regulatory requirements. Its policy system, spanning VCF Operations and VCF Automation, enables hierarchical customization and ranked policy processing, ensuring that configuration deviations are managed according to defined organizational standards and applied consistently across different workload types or mission functions.

Similarly, vDefend offers extensive mechanisms for tailoring network security baselines through its Distributed Firewall, federated policy management, and Security Services Platform, allowing for granular control over security policies, segmentation, and threat response based on specific operational contexts and application requirements.

While VCF and vDefend provide the foundational framework for managing and enforcing configuration baselines and the application of changes, the platform's described capabilities do not directly include mechanisms for verifying the identity of an external software source prior to the introduction of a change that deviates from an existing baseline.

The responsibility for establishing and executing processes to verify the authenticity and integrity of software sources, such as through digital signatures, trusted repositories, or other supply chain security measures, rests with the customer. This ensures that only authorized and verified software changes are introduced into the VCF environment for subsequent baseline management and application.

Description:
Prior to a change that deviates from the existing baseline configuration associated with baseline items in Parts 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.5, and when the method to do so is available to the Responsible Entity from the software source:

1.6.2. Verify the integrity of the software obtained from the software source.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for defining, managing, and enforcing security baselines across the environment. The VCF 9 Security Baseline offers a consolidated, out-of-the-box benchmark, and organizations can tailor these baselines using hierarchical policy systems in VCF Operations and Automation. These systems allow for the creation of custom policies, inheritance from base policies, and ranked processing to ensure that configurations align with defined security postures. VCF also supports the application of industry-standard benchmarks such as DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs), further solidifying its role in maintaining configuration integrity.

VMware vDefend extends this by providing extensive mechanisms for tailoring network security baselines, including the Distributed Firewall, federated policy management, and advanced threat detection capabilities like IDS/IPS and context profiles. These features ensure that the network security posture, which is a critical component of the overall baseline, is consistently managed and enforced.

While VCF and vDefend provide comprehensive tools for managing and enforcing the integrity of *configurations* and *baselines* within the environment, the direct verification of the integrity of *software packages obtained from external sources* (e.g., through cryptographic hashes or digital signatures) remains a customer responsibility. The platform's capabilities establish the necessary framework for maintaining a secure baseline, thereby requiring that any software introduced to modify this baseline undergo external integrity verification to ensure the overall security posture is not compromised.

Description:
Monitor at least once every 35 calendar
days for changes to the baseline
configuration (as described in Requirement
R1, Part 1.1). Document and investigate
detected unauthorized changes.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for monitoring changes to baseline configurations through VCF Operations, which includes compliance scanning tools designed to evaluate infrastructure against established baselines. These tools facilitate ongoing assessment and identification of configuration drift across all SDDC components, leveraging benchmarks such as the VCF 9 Security Baseline. VCF's integrated lifecycle management also performs automatic post-upgrade reconciliation of configuration drift fixes.

VMware vDefend further contributes by providing configuration integrity controls within the network security domain. Its Security Services Platform includes a Security Assessment capability that evaluates workload and application security posture against configurable baseline requirements, recording detected issues with relevant identifiers. The Security Intelligence Policy Recommendations capability analyzes observed traffic to generate and refresh firewall rule recommendations, which can be rerun to detect changes and support periodic review cycles. vDefend's structured change management, including draft configuration management with locking and pre-deployment validation, helps maintain configuration integrity and reduce unauthorized changes.

While the platform provides the necessary technical tools for monitoring and detecting configuration changes, the organization is responsible for establishing and enforcing the operational cadence to ensure monitoring occurs at least once every 35 calendar days. The organization must also define and implement the processes for documenting and investigating any detected unauthorized changes, including assigning personnel, maintaining records, and determining appropriate remediation actions.

Description:
At least once every 15 calendar months,
conduct a paper or active vulnerability
assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for vulnerability detection and compliance scanning within its own infrastructure stack. VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically identifies known issues and security vulnerabilities across the VCF software stack by scanning against VMware best practices, logs, and properties, providing detailed descriptions and remediation steps. The Security Operations dashboard consolidates user and infrastructure security elements, highlighting security exposure and identifying vulnerable operating systems, including end-of-life platforms. PowerCLI's `Test-Compliance` command scans entities for missing security updates, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager tracks host compliance against desired cluster images. VCF Operations Diagnostic Findings offers a consolidated view of VMSA-based security exposures alongside best-practice recommendations. VMware vDefend further contributes to vulnerability awareness through its Security Intelligence Security Segmentation Report, which identifies vulnerable workloads and provides an overall view of security posture. Its IDS/IPS threat detection records security issues, including CVSS scores and attack details, and the Security Services Platform security explorer visually represents the security posture within the vDefend-managed environment.

While VCF and vDefend offer extensive tools for platform-level and workload-level security insights, they do not perform comprehensive host-based or application-layer vulnerability scanning for guest operating systems and applications running within the environment. To fully satisfy the requirement for a complete vulnerability assessment, customers must integrate dedicated third-party vulnerability scanning tools. VCF's REST APIs and PowerCLI support facilitate integration with external scanners and security information and event management (SIEM) platforms to achieve this broader coverage. The customer is responsible for defining the scope, frequency, and methodology of these assessments, as well as the processes for managing and remediating identified vulnerabilities.

Description:
Where technically feasible, at least once every 36 calendar months:
3.2.1 Perform an active vulnerability assessment in a test environment, or perform an active vulnerability assessment in a production environment where the test is performed in a manner that minimizes adverse effects, that models the baseline configuration of the BES Cyber System in a production environment; and
3.2.2 Document the results of the testing and, if a test environment was used, the differences between the test environment and the production environment, including a description of the measures used to account for any differences in operation between the test and production environments.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for vulnerability detection and compliance scanning within its own infrastructure stack. VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically identifies known issues and security vulnerabilities across the VCF software, scanning against VMware best practices and providing detailed remediation steps. The Security Operations dashboard offers a consolidated view of user and infrastructure security, highlighting exposure and identifying vulnerable operating systems, including end-of-life platforms. For patch compliance, PowerCLI's Test-Compliance command scans entities for missing security updates, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager tracks host compliance against desired cluster image specifications, reporting any drift. VCF Operations Diagnostic Findings also consolidates VMSA-based security exposures with best-practice recommendations.

VMware vDefend further contributes to vulnerability awareness through its Security Intelligence Security Segmentation Report, which identifies vulnerable workloads and provides an overall security posture view. Its IDS/IPS threat detection records security issues, including CVSS scores and attack details, and the Security Services Platform security explorer visually represents the security posture within the vDefend-managed environment.

While VCF and vDefend offer significant capabilities for the underlying infrastructure and workload visibility, they do not perform host-based or application-layer vulnerability scanning for guest operating systems and applications running on the platform.

Satisfying the requirement for active vulnerability assessments of the entire BES Cyber System, including guest OS and applications, necessitates the integration of dedicated third-party vulnerability scanning tools.

The customer is responsible for establishing the processes for performing these active vulnerability assessments, whether in a test or production environment, ensuring tests minimize adverse effects, modeling the baseline configuration of the BES Cyber System, and comprehensively documenting the assessment results, including any differences between test and production environments and measures taken to account for operational variations.

Description:
Where technically feasible, at least once every 36 calendar months:
3.2.1 Perform an active vulnerability assessment in a test environment, or perform an active vulnerability assessment in a production environment where the test is performed in a manner that minimizes adverse effects, that models the baseline configuration of the BES Cyber System in a production environment; and
3.2.2 Document the results of the testing and, if a test environment was used, the differences between the test environment and the production environment, including a description of the measures used to account for any differences in operation between the test and production environments.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for identifying vulnerabilities within its own infrastructure stack. VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically detects known issues and security vulnerabilities, scanning the platform against VMware best practices and providing detailed descriptions and remediation steps.

The Security Operations dashboard consolidates security elements, highlighting exposure across the environment and identifying vulnerable operating systems.

Patch compliance scanning is supported via PowerCLI's Test-Compliance command, which identifies missing security updates, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager tracks host compliance against desired cluster images. VCF Operations Diagnostic Findings also provides a consolidated view of VMSA-based security exposures.

VMware vDefend enhances vulnerability awareness through its Security Intelligence Security Segmentation Report, offering an overall view of security posture and identifying vulnerable workloads. Its IDS/IPS threat detection records security issues, including CVSS scores and attack details, while the Security Services Platform security explorer visually represents the security posture.

While these platforms provide critical data and insights into the security posture and detected vulnerabilities within the VCF stack and managed environments, the comprehensive documentation of vulnerability assessment results, including differences between test and production environments and measures to account for operational variances, remains a customer responsibility. Dedicated vulnerability scanning tools are required for host-based and application-layer scanning, and the customer must integrate these tools, perform the assessments, analyze the findings, and formalize the documentation process to fully satisfy the control requirements.

Description:
Prior to adding a new applicable Cyber
Asset to a production environment,
perform an active vulnerability
assessment of the new Cyber Asset,
except for CIP Exceptional Circumstances
and like replacements of the same type of
Cyber Asset with a baseline configuration
that models an existing baseline
configuration of the previous or other
existing Cyber Asset.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for active vulnerability assessment of its own infrastructure stack and managed components. VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically detects known issues and security vulnerabilities within the VCF software stack, scanning the platform against VMware best practices, logs, and properties to identify diagnostic findings. The Security Operations dashboard consolidates security elements, highlighting exposure and identifying vulnerable operating systems within the managed infrastructure, including end-of-life platforms. For patch compliance, PowerCLI's `Test-Compliance` command scans entities against downloaded patches, identifying missing security updates, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager tracks host drift from desired states. VCF Operations Diagnostic Findings also provides a consolidated view of VMSA-based security exposures. VMware vDefend further contributes to vulnerability awareness through its Security Intelligence Security Segmentation Report, which identifies vulnerable workloads and provides an overall security posture view, and through IDS/IPS threat detection that records security issues with CVSS scores and attack details.

While VCF provides comprehensive vulnerability detection and compliance scanning for its own infrastructure stack, performing active vulnerability assessments of guest operating systems and applications running on VCF requires integration with dedicated third-party vulnerability scanning tools. vDefend does not perform host-based or application-layer vulnerability scanning. Customers must define the scope of "applicable Cyber Assets" beyond the VCF infrastructure, establish the processes for performing these assessments prior to adding new assets to production, and integrate external scanning solutions using VCF's REST APIs and PowerCLI support to achieve full coverage for all workloads.

Description:
Document the results of the assessments
conducted according to Parts 3.1, 3.2, and
3.3 and the action plan to remediate or
mitigate vulnerabilities identified in the
assessments including the planned date of
completing the action plan and the
execution status of any remediation or
mitigation action items.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for identifying vulnerabilities and informing remediation efforts within its infrastructure stack. VCF Operations Diagnostics automatically detects known issues and security vulnerabilities, offering detailed descriptions, root cause insights, and actionable remediation steps. The Security Operations dashboard consolidates security elements, highlighting exposure and identifying vulnerable operating systems, including end-of-life platforms. Patch compliance scanning is performed through PowerCLI's Test-Compliance command and vSphere Lifecycle Manager, which tracks host state against desired cluster images. VCF Operations Diagnostic Findings provides a consolidated view of VMSA-based security exposures alongside best-practice recommendations. VMware vDefend further contributes to vulnerability awareness through its Security Intelligence Security Segmentation Report, which identifies vulnerable workloads and provides an overall view of security posture. Its IDS/IPS threat detection records detected security issues, including CVSS scores and attack details, and the Security Services Platform security explorer visually represents security posture. These platforms generate critical data and insights that serve as the foundation for documenting assessment results and informing remediation strategies.

While VCF and vDefend provide the technical findings and remediation guidance, they do not inherently provide a formal system for documenting the aggregated results of all assessments, including those from third-party tools for guest operating systems and applications. The customer is responsible for establishing and maintaining a comprehensive action plan to remediate or mitigate identified vulnerabilities, including defining planned completion dates and tracking the execution status of all remediation or mitigation action items. This requires customer-defined processes and potentially external tools to serve as the system of record for formal documentation and ongoing status management.

Description: Method(s) to identify BCSI.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust methods for identifying and inventorying technology assets across compute, storage, and networking layers. vCenter maintains the authoritative inventory of all managed objects, including ESX hosts, clusters, virtual machines, datastores, and networking constructs, with programmatic access available via the vSphere Web Services API. VCF Operations retrieves and logically groups these discovered objects, presenting them in detailed inventory lists and interactive dashboards. The VCF Operations Service Discovery adapter identifies running applications and services, allowing for grouping and export. VCF Automation tracks provisioned resources through its project model, providing scoped visibility for audit and review. vSphere Lifecycle Manager maintains a software depot, tracking installed components and available updates across the environment.

Integration with external asset registries is supported through the Management Pack for ServiceNow, which populates the ServiceNow CMDB with discovered resources, and VCF Automation supports CMDB reconciliation to keep configuration items current. VMware vDefend further contributes by maintaining an inventory of network-visible workloads, organized into asset collections spanning groups, infrastructure assets, environments, applications, and application tiers, supporting granular tracking of application-layer assets.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide comprehensive technical asset identification and inventory capabilities, the enterprise-level classification of these assets as containing or processing Bulk Cyber Security Information (BCSI), along with business justification tracking and property accountability processes, remains an organizational responsibility. vDefend's inventory scope is limited to network-visible workloads and does not replace broader enterprise-wide asset inventory tools, authorized software catalogs with business justification records, or data asset registries required for full compliance.

Description:
Method(s) to protect and securely
handle BCSI to mitigate risks of
compromising confidentiality.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive technical methods to protect and securely handle sensitive information, mitigating risks of compromising confidentiality.

Data at rest is protected through vSAN encryption and per-VM encryption, both utilizing FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules and integrating with external Key Management Systems. All data in transit between VCF components is secured with TLS.

VMware Live Site Recovery extends data protection with replication-based capabilities, including encryption of replication traffic, orchestrated disaster recovery plans, and configurable recovery point objectives.

VMware vDefend enhances this by providing network-level security, enforcing microsegmentation with the Distributed Firewall to restrict unauthorized network access to systems containing sensitive data and preventing lateral movement. vDefend's Security Intelligence identifies risky clear-text application protocols and detects unusual communication patterns indicative of data exfiltration. Its IDS/IPS inspects east-west traffic for data theft signatures, and Malware Prevention analyzes files for malicious payloads.

VCF Operations further controls access to collected operational data through privilege-based access controls.

While the platform delivers these robust technical controls for data protection, secure handling, and network security, the organization remains responsible for defining its specific Bulk Cyber Security Information (BCSI) assets, establishing comprehensive policies for their secure handling, configuring the platform's capabilities to align with these policies, and maintaining the overall governance framework. This includes defining ownership, lifecycle management, and human accountability for BCSI.

Description:
Prior to the release for reuse of
applicable Cyber Assets that contain
BCSI (except for reuse within other
systems identified in the “Applicable
Systems” column), the Responsible
Entity shall take action to prevent the
unauthorized retrieval of BCSI from
the Cyber Asset data storage media.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for media sanitization, directly addressing the prevention of unauthorized data retrieval from cyber asset storage media prior to reuse.

vSAN includes features such as "Wipe residual data" and, when encryption is enabled, an "Erase disks before use" option, ensuring secure data removal from storage devices upon onboarding or decommissioning.

For environments leveraging vSAN data-at-rest encryption or VM Encryption, cryptographic erasure is a primary method, where the destruction of encryption keys renders the underlying data unrecoverable without requiring physical overwriting. VCF's key management infrastructure, supporting KMIP-compatible external Key Management Servers and vSphere Native Key Provider, facilitates these cryptographic erasure processes through key removal and rotation APIs.

Furthermore, ESX host configuration data, stored encrypted on boot devices and protected by TPM 2.0, can be rendered unrecoverable by clearing the TPM during host decommissioning. VCF also enforces secure deletion practices for its own operational data, such as decrypted backup files for SDDC Manager, vCenter, and NSX after restore operations.

The "No Cryptography Administrator" role further enhances security by separating cryptographic privileges, preventing unauthorized modifications to encryption settings during media lifecycle events.

While VCF provides these critical technical mechanisms, it does not encompass dedicated media sanitization workflow tools, inventory tracking for media disposal, or comprehensive chain-of-custody documentation capabilities. These organizational processes, along with physical media destruction procedures, remain the responsibility of the customer and must be integrated into their broader enterprise asset management and data governance programs.

Description:
Each Responsible Entity shall implement one or more documented information protection program(s) for BES Cyber System
Information (BCSI) pertaining to “Applicable Systems” identified in CIP-011-3 Table R1 – Information Protection Program
that collectively includes each of the applicable requirement parts in CIP-011-3 Table R1 – Information Protection Program.
[Violation Risk Factor: Medium] [Time Horizon: Operations Planning].
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities that contribute to the implementation of information protection programs for BES Cyber System Information (BCSI) by establishing a strong foundation for IT Asset Management.

VCF includes built-in inventory, discovery, and integration features, enabling the logical grouping of discovered objects and providing detailed inventory lists across compute, storage, and networking resources. The Service Discovery adapter automatically identifies applications, services, and their associated metrics running on managed infrastructure, with the ability to export this data for external asset tracking systems.

For organizations utilizing ServiceNow, VCF offers direct integration paths, including a Management Pack for VCF Operations that populates the ServiceNow CMDB, and VCF Automation's extensibility action scripts and dedicated ITSM Plug-in for CMDB synchronization and day-2 resource action support. Furthermore, VCF Automation enhances governance through projects that link users, catalog items, and IaaS resources, along with policies governing provisioned resources such as VMs and Kubernetes clusters. Tags and Day 2 Action policies within VCF Automation control access and management actions, providing granular control over the environment.

While VCF delivers essential technical tools for asset identification, inventory, and operational governance, the broader responsibilities for establishing comprehensive asset governance policies, defining information protection program elements, outlining ownership lifecycles, and maintaining organizational accountability for BES Cyber System Information remain outside the platform's scope. These programmatic and policy-driven aspects must be defined, documented, and enforced by the responsible entity to fully satisfy the requirements of an information protection program.

Description:
One or more process(es) used in procuring BES Cyber Systems, and their associated EACMS and PACS, that address the following, as applicable:

1.2.3. Notification by vendors when remote or onsite access should no longer be granted to vendor representatives;
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive capabilities for revoking logical access authorizations across its integrated stack.

VCF Automation allows administrators to revoke individual user access tokens and disable user accounts, immediately preventing further programmatic or interactive access. vCenter's AuthorizationManager offers API methods such as RemoveEntityPermission and ResetEntityPermissions to revoke access to specific vSphere inventory objects, complemented by the accessControls API for managing ACLs. Role-based access control across vCenter, ESX, NSX, and SDDC Manager supports granular permission removal, and centralized authentication through VCF SSO ensures that disabling or removing a user's identity source account effectively revokes access across all VCF components simultaneously. For Kubernetes workloads, namespace-level role bindings can be removed to revoke access to specific environments.

VMware vDefend further enhances access revocation through network-level controls that can immediately restrict or terminate connectivity. Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall policies can be updated to deny network access to specific workloads, IP addresses, or segments, with changes taking effect immediately at the vNIC level or tier-0/tier-1 gateway. Identity Firewall integrates with Active Directory, recognizing changes in AD group membership to adjust network access, making revocation as straightforward as removing a user from a relevant AD group. Microsegmentation policies provide an additional mechanism, allowing fine-grained authorization policies to be updated to isolate workloads or deny traffic flows, providing immediate network-level enforcement of revocation decisions.

The establishment of a formal procurement process that mandates vendor notification regarding the cessation of access for their representatives, and the subsequent management of such notifications, remains an organizational policy and procedural responsibility. While the platform provides the robust technical mechanisms to execute access revocation in response to such notifications, it does not inherently define or enforce the vendor notification process itself.
